title: Brunei Darussalam — GTCI 2025: Priority Sector Analysis
description: Five priority sectors aligned to Wawasan 2035 economic diversification strategy.

sectors.title: ⚡  SECTOR 1: DOWNSTREAM O&G, ENERGY TRANSITION & INNOVATION
sectors.context: Brunei's economy is built on hydrocarbon wealth but must transition toward downstream value-added activities and renewable energy. O&G contributes ~60% of government revenue. GTCI indicators most relevant here are those affecting: investor confidence (governance), research capacity (R&D, researcher density), environmental performance (energy transition readiness), and knowledge outputs (IP, publications, exports). Improving these indicators signals to global investors that Brunei is a credible long-term O&G and clean energy partner.
sectors.wawasan_ref: Wawasan 2035 — Economic Pillar | Brunei National Development Plan | Ministry of Development Energy White Paper
sectors.sg_benchmark: Singapore ranks #1 globally (GTCI 2025). Brunei ranks #43. Singapore's O&G and clean energy ecosystem is driven by a world-class R&D base (rank 4 in researcher density) — the key capability Brunei must build.
sectors.sector_target: Improve average sector indicator score from 38/100 to 52/100 by 2030. Priority: R&D expenditure reporting, researcher density, IP receipts, and environmental performance.
sectors.new_indicators: Added 4.1.5 (workforce quality), 3.3.1 (management), and 1.3.1 (labour rights) as additional indicators relevant to O&G sector workforce competitiveness and investor attraction.

indicators.name: Government Effectiveness
indicators.definition:
Perceptions of the quality of public services, the quality of the civil service and the degree of its independence from political pressures, the quality of policy formulation and implementation, and the credibility of the government's commitment to such policies. Score 0–100; higher = more effective.

Official GTCI Data Source: World Bank WGI — Government Effectiveness Estimate; data.worldbank.org/indicator/GE.EST
indicators.primary_source: World Bank, The Worldwide Governance Indicators
indicators.why_relevant: The WGI Government Effectiveness indicator directly captures civil service quality and policy delivery capacity — foundational for attracting downstream O&G investment and executing energy transition plans. International energy companies demand credible regulatory implementation (licensing, permitting, environmental compliance) before committing capital. Brunei's rank 17/135 signals a professional public sector that can manage complex energy project governance.
indicators.remarks: Score 80.06/100 (rank 17/135). Genuine governance strength. Maintain by: (1) submitting timely WGI data annually via BEDB/MOF coordination with World Bank (data.worldbank.org); (2) briefing WEF EOS respondents on energy sector regulatory delivery improvements — BDDB licensing reforms and PMB Industrial Zone streamlining. Gap to Singapore (97.0, rank 2): 16.9 pts — reduce through civil service digitalisation and faster energy-sector permit-to-operate timelines.

indicators.name: Rule of Law
indicators.definition:
Captures perceptions of the extent to which agents have confidence in and abide by the rules of society — in particular contract enforcement, property rights, police, courts — as well as the likelihood of crime and violence. Score 0–100; higher = stronger rule of law.

Official GTCI Data Source: World Bank WGI — Rule of Law Estimate; data.worldbank.org/indicator/RL.EST
indicators.why_relevant: WGI Rule of Law captures contract enforcement, property rights quality, and judicial reliability — the legal bedrock for long-term O&G investment contracts, joint ventures, and Production Sharing Contracts (PSCs). International energy majors require certainty that contracts will be upheld and disputes resolved predictably. Brunei's rank 29/135 reflects a functioning English common law system, though commercial court speed and IP enforcement remain improvement areas relevant to downstream energy agreements.
indicators.remarks: Score 73.77/100 (rank 29/135). Strong for a small economy. Energy sector relevance: Shell Brunei and BSP's investment confidence rests partly on Brunei's contractual reliability. To improve: (1) publish commercial contract resolution timeline data; (2) strengthen Brunei International Arbitration Centre infrastructure to provide fast-track energy dispute resolution; (3) engage World Bank Doing Business surveys annually to feed WGI indirectly.

indicators.name: Political Stability
indicators.definition:
Measures perceptions of the likelihood of political instability and/or politically-motivated violence, including terrorism. Score 0–100; higher = more stable.

Official GTCI Data Source: World Bank WGI — Political Stability & Absence of Violence; data.worldbank.org/indicator/PV.EST
indicators.why_relevant: WGI Political Stability measures likelihood of politically-motivated disruption — the single most critical governance factor for energy sector FDI. Multinational O&G companies and LNG buyers require sovereign stability before committing 20–30 year capital investments. Brunei's rank 2/135 is an extraordinary competitive advantage providing near-zero political risk — the country's primary geopolitical selling point to international energy partners and LNG off-takers.
indicators.remarks: Score 98.6/100 (rank 2/135) — Brunei's highest GTCI indicator and the bedrock of its O&G investment proposition. Feature explicitly in BEDB and Ministry of Energy investment promotion materials. Risk management: GTCI scores may fluctuate due to methodology changes, not actual instability — MOE/BEDB should monitor WGI annual updates (data.worldbank.org) and engage World Bank directly if scores unexpectedly decline.

indicators.name: Regulatory Quality
indicators.definition:
Captures perceptions of the ability of the government to formulate and implement sound policies and regulations that permit and promote private sector development. Score 0–100; higher = better regulatory quality.

Official GTCI Data Source: World Bank WGI — Regulatory Quality Estimate; data.worldbank.org/indicator/RQ.EST
indicators.why_relevant: WGI Regulatory Quality captures government capacity to implement sound private-sector regulations — directly relevant to O&G's need for predictable permitting, environmental compliance regimes, and downstream manufacturing licences at PMB Industrial Zone. Low regulatory quality increases compliance costs and deters downstream value-added investment. Brunei's rank 31/135 is moderate; the 24.8-point gap to Singapore signals specific process bottlenecks in energy sector approvals.
indicators.remarks: Score 69.28/100 (rank 31/135). Gap to Singapore (94.1, rank 3): 24.8 pts. Key improvement areas: (1) streamline BDDB foreign business licence approvals for energy sector — target <5 business days vs current 15–30 days; (2) publish a transparent regulatory roadmap for clean energy (solar, hydrogen) licensing with defined timelines; (3) adopt OECD Regulatory Policy Toolkit standards for energy-sector regulatory impact assessments.

indicators.name: R&D Expenditure
indicators.definition:
Gross domestic expenditure on research and development (GERD) as a percentage of GDP. Covers basic research, applied research, and experimental development by all institutional sectors.

Official GTCI Data Source: UNESCO UIS R&D Survey (Form S); WIPO GII; OECD MSTI — uis.unesco.org
indicators.primary_source: UNESCO Institute for Statistics, WIPO GII, OECD MSTI
indicators.why_relevant: UNESCO GERD (Gross Domestic Expenditure on R&D as % GDP) captures national investment in knowledge creation — the engine of energy technology innovation. For Brunei's O&G transition, R&D investment is decisive: countries developing proprietary enhanced oil recovery (EOR), carbon capture (CCS), hydrogen production, and renewable energy technologies gain durable competitive advantage. Brunei at 4.29/100 (rank 80) is critically underinvested — the downstream transition to petrochemicals, LNG optimisation, and clean energy cannot proceed without a funded R&D base.
indicators.remarks: Score 4.29/100 (rank 80/135). Estimated R&D spend ~0.1% GDP — far below ASEAN average (~1.0%) and UNESCO minimum recommendation. Actions: (1) Conduct National R&D Expenditure Audit covering UBD, UTB, Shell Brunei Technology Centre, and EVPMO — existing spend is not fully reported to UNESCO UIS Form S; (2) Submit to UNESCO UIS (uis.stats@unesco.org) by June annually; (3) Establish National R&D Fund targeting 0.5% GDP by 2028, focused on O&G downstream and clean energy; (4) Introduce 150% private-sector R&D tax credit through MoFE.

indicators.name: FDI Regulatory Restrictiveness
indicators.definition:
Measures statutory restrictions on FDI across 22 sectors. OECD index: 0=open, 1=closed; inverted in GTCI so higher score = more open to FDI.

Official GTCI Data Source: OECD FDI Regulatory Restrictiveness Index; oecd.org/investment/fdiindex.htm
indicators.why_relevant: The OECD FDI Restrictiveness Index directly measures statutory barriers to foreign investment across 22 sectors — critical for attracting downstream O&G capital from international majors and clean energy developers. The OECD framework assesses equity limitations, screening requirements, restrictions on key foreign personnel, and operational constraints. Restrictive FDI rules in energy services and downstream manufacturing directly suppress the capital inflows Brunei needs for PMB Industrial Zone development.
indicators.remarks: Score 54.68/100 (rank 77/135). Key O&G FDI actions: (1) Respond annually to OECD FDI Restrictiveness Questionnaire (oecd.org/investment/fdiindex.htm); (2) Publish FDI liberalisation roadmap for downstream O&G and clean energy — reduce restrictions in oil refining services, LNG terminal operations, and renewable energy project development; (3) Allow 100% foreign equity for clean energy power generation under an amended Investment Promotions Act.

indicators.name: Environmental Performance
indicators.definition:
Composite score across 40 environmental performance indicators in 11 issue categories covering ecosystem vitality and environmental health (air quality, water, biodiversity, forests, fisheries, climate, energy, waste, agriculture). Score 0–100.

Official GTCI Data Source: Yale Center for Environmental Law & Policy — Environmental Performance Index (EPI) 2024; epi.yale.edu
indicators.primary_source: Yale Center for Environmental Law & Policy EPI 2024
indicators.why_relevant: Yale EPI covers 40 indicators across 11 categories including air quality, carbon intensity, ecosystem vitality, and energy transition readiness. For Brunei's O&G sector, EPI reflects how successfully the country manages hydrocarbon extraction's environmental impact while transitioning to cleaner energy. International LNG buyers and ESG-focused investors increasingly screen against EPI scores. Brunei's 48.3/100 (rank 55) is depressed by high carbon intensity from the O&G sector, undermining ESG narratives in export markets.
indicators.remarks: Score 48.3/100 (rank 55/135). Gap to Singapore (58.1, rank 39): 9.8 pts. Actions: (1) Submit annual environmental data to Yale EPI team (epi.yale.edu) before January data lock; (2) Accelerate solar energy deployment to reduce O&G sector carbon intensity — 2025 target of 100MW solar; (3) Publish Brunei Long-Term Low Emissions Development Strategy aligned with Paris Agreement; (4) Submit air quality data from Seria/Kuala Belait O&G operations to WHO Global Air Quality Database — targeted quick score improvement.

indicators.name: Researchers, Full-time equivalent
indicators.definition:
Full-time equivalent (FTE) researchers per 1,000 persons in the labour force. Researchers are professionals engaged in creation of new knowledge, products, processes, methods and systems.

Official GTCI Data Source: UNESCO UIS Form R / WIPO GII / OECD MSTI — Researcher FTE Density; uis.unesco.org
indicators.primary_source:
Global Innovation Index Databas, WIPO 2025.
UNESCO Institute for Statistics (UIS)
OECD, Main Science and Technology Indicators (MSTI) Database
Ibero-American and Inter-American Network of Science and Technology Indicators (RICYT)
indicators.why_relevant: UNESCO FTE researcher density (researchers per 1,000 labour force) captures the scientific human capital needed to generate applied energy technology. Without researchers, Brunei cannot develop proprietary EOR, CCS, green hydrogen, or bio-fuel technologies. Brunei at 4.82/100 (rank 73) translates to an estimated 20–25 researchers nationally. The O&G sector's transition from extraction to innovation requires a research talent base that does not yet exist at the needed scale.
indicators.remarks: Score 4.82/100 (rank 73/135). Critical gap. Actions: (1) Conduct National Researcher Census covering UBD, UTB, UNISSA, Shell Technology Centre, BSRG, and BDFA — submit to UNESCO UIS Form R (uis.stats@unesco.org) by June annually; (2) Establish 20 energy-focused Postdoctoral Fellowships (BND 5,000/month × 2 years) at UBD Energy Institute; (3) Create Research Talent Pass — 5-year visa for energy research PhDs; (4) Establish Brunei Research Council (BRC) to coordinate UNESCO researcher data submissions.

indicators.name: Intellectual Property Receipts
indicators.definition:
Charges for the use of intellectual property received from abroad (royalties, patent licensing, franchise fees) as a percentage of total trade. Measures capacity to generate commercially valuable IP and license it internationally.

Official GTCI Data Source: IMF Balance of Payments Statistics / WTO Trade Database; imf.org/en/Data
indicators.primary_source: IMF Balance of Payments Statistics / WTO Trade Database
indicators.why_relevant: IMF BOP IP receipts (royalties, patent licensing, franchise fees as % of total trade) measure the financial return on a country's innovation output. For the O&G sector, IP receipts could arise from proprietary drilling technology, EOR processes, LNG safety systems, or clean energy innovations developed in Brunei. Currently zero because no commercial IP ecosystem exists. For Brunei to become a downstream energy innovation hub, IP commercialisation is the required revenue model.
indicators.remarks: Score 0/100 (rank 121/135) — Brunei earns zero IP receipts from abroad. O&G sector IP actions: (1) BruIPO (bruipo.gov.bn) to launch patent filing support for Shell Brunei Technology Centre and UBD energy researchers — file process patents before publishing research; (2) Introduce Patent Box tax regime (0–5% tax on IP income) to attract energy technology IP holding companies; (3) BDCB to submit comprehensive IP receipts to IMF BOP database (imf.org/en/Data); (4) 10-year target: BND 10M in annual IP licensing income from energy technology sector.

indicators.name: High-Value Exports
indicators.definition:
Medium-high and high-technology manufactured exports plus knowledge-intensive service exports as a percentage of total exports. Measures technological sophistication and knowledge intensity of a country's export base.

Official GTCI Data Source: UN Comtrade / World Bank WDI / OECD Technology Intensity Classification; comtrade.un.org
indicators.primary_source: UN Comtrade / World Bank WDI / OECD classification
indicators.why_relevant: UN Comtrade / OECD WDI measures medium-high and high-technology exports plus knowledge-intensive services as % of total exports. For Brunei's O&G sector, this captures whether the country has moved beyond raw LNG/crude export toward downstream value-added products — methanol, urea fertilisers, petrochemicals, LNG derivatives, and refined petroleum products. Brunei at 1.53/100 (rank 115) is almost entirely dependent on raw commodity exports — the transition to high-value energy exports is the defining economic challenge of Wawasan 2035.
indicators.remarks: Score 1.53/100 (rank 115/135). O&G downstream actions: (1) Conduct UN Comtrade export classification audit — verify that downstream petroleum products, methanol, and LNG-derived chemicals are correctly coded under OECD high-tech HS codes (chapters 27–29 for petrochemicals); (2) Develop PMB Industrial Zone downstream anchor investment targeting methanol plant, urea plant, or speciality chemicals facility by 2030; (3) Submit revised export classification data to UN Comtrade (comtrade.un.org) and World Bank before annual cutoff.

indicators.name: Scientific Journal Articles
indicators.definition:
Citable scientific journal articles (Scopus / Web of Science) per million population. Measures quantity of peer-reviewed knowledge output from a country's research system.

Official GTCI Data Source: UNESCO Science Report / SCImago–Scopus / World Bank WDI; uis.unesco.org
indicators.primary_source:
UNESCO Science Report / Web of Science / Scopus
World Bank World Development Indicators based on National Science Foundation, Science and Engineering Indicators
indicators.why_relevant: SCImago/Scopus publications per million population — the primary output metric of a functioning research system. For O&G and energy transition, publications signal a country's capacity to generate applicable knowledge in petroleum engineering, chemical engineering, materials science, and environmental science. Brunei at 32.91/100 (rank 37) shows an improving research base led by UBD — but the energy sector specifically needs more applied O&G and clean energy publications to build credibility as an energy innovation partner.
indicators.remarks: Score 32.91/100 (rank 37/135) — improving +8.62 pts. Brunei's best knowledge output indicator. Accelerate: (1) Establish APC (Article Processing Charge) Fund of BND 500,000/year for open-access energy research publications at UBD (chemistry, engineering, environmental science); (2) UBD to reconcile 'UBD' and 'Universiti Brunei Darussalam' affiliation variants in Scopus — missing publications from name variants; (3) Submit full publication list to UNESCO UIS annually; (4) Target 20 new international research MOUs with energy faculties in Malaysia, UK, Australia, and Norway.

indicators.name: Vulnerable Employment
indicators.definition:
Share of total employment in non-vulnerable jobs — complement of vulnerable employment (own-account workers + contributing family workers as % of total employment). Score 0–100; higher = more formal, secure employment.

Official GTCI Data Source: ILO ILOSTAT — Vulnerable Employment Rate (inverted); ilostat.ilo.org
indicators.why_relevant: ILO defines vulnerable employment as own-account workers + contributing family workers — those without stable contracts or social protection. Brunei's 95.02/100 (rank 10) means 95% of the workforce is in formal, protected employment. This signals to international energy companies that Brunei's O&G workforce is stable, professionally contracted, and ILO-compliant — a critical ESG and operational risk factor for multinational operators required to meet ILO standards in their global supply chains.
indicators.remarks: Score 95.02/100 (rank 10/135) — competitive strength. Maintain by: (1) submitting annual LFS employment-status data to ILO ILOSTAT (ilostat@ilo.org); (2) highlighting this rank in BEDB investment promotion materials as evidence of a formally employed O&G workforce; (3) ensuring migrant workers in O&G support services (construction, catering) are captured as formal employees in LFS data — avoiding under-counting that could mask genuine precarious employment.

indicators.name: Delegation of Authority
indicators.definition:
Measures extent to which authority is delegated to employees. WEF EOS: 'To what extent do companies delegate authority to employees?' (1=not at all; 7=to a great extent). Normalised 0–100.

Official GTCI Data Source: WEF Executive Opinion Survey 2025; reports.weforum.org
indicators.why_relevant: WEF EOS measures extent to which companies delegate decision-making authority to employees — a proxy for management culture maturity and operational agility. For O&G operations, delegation is safety-critical: multi-site drilling, pipeline management, and offshore operations require front-line workers to make rapid independent decisions without hierarchical delays. Brunei's rank 26/135 indicates a management culture that is delegation-friendly — directly relevant to safe, efficient O&G field operations.
indicators.remarks: Score 65.69/100 (rank 26/135). Leverage: (1) Include this positive rank in BEDB O&G investment pitch materials — signals a management-capable workforce; (2) Brief WEF EOS respondents via annual BEDB pre-survey pack on BSP and Shell Brunei's empowered management practices as exemplars; (3) Expand management delegation training through CSPS to O&G sector GLCs — maintaining this strength as the sector grows.

indicators.name: Labour Rights
indicators.definition:
Degree to which labour rights are respected and enforced, covering freedom of association, right to collective bargaining, and prohibition of forced and child labour. Score 0–100; higher = better rights protection.

Official GTCI Data Source: ILO ILOSTAT — Labour Rights Compliance Indicators; ilostat.ilo.org
indicators.why_relevant: ILO measures compliance with core labour standards — freedom of association, collective bargaining, prohibition of forced and child labour, non-discrimination. For international O&G investors and LNG buyers, particularly from Europe and North America, ILO compliance is an ESG screening criterion. EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanisms and OECD Due Diligence Guidelines increasingly require supply chain labour rights verification from energy exporting countries. Brunei's rank 83/135 is adequate but freedom-of-association gaps create potential ESG risk for international energy partnerships.
indicators.remarks: Score 74.2/100 (rank 83/135). Gap to Singapore (80.5, rank 12): 6.3 pts. Actions: (1) Submit annual labour rights compliance data to ILO ILOSTAT (ilostat.ilo.org) comprehensively; (2) Review Trade Unions Act alignment with ILO Convention No. 87 (Freedom of Association) — particularly relevant for migrant O&G support workers; (3) Publish annual O&G Sector Worker Welfare Report to pre-empt ESG scrutiny from European energy partners; (4) Ratify remaining ILO core conventions to directly improve the score.

sectors.title: 🌿  SECTOR 2: HALAL FOOD INDUSTRY & AGRI-BUSINESS
sectors.context: Brunei has a globally recognised halal brand and aspires to be a leading halal economy in ASEAN. The halal food, pharmaceutical, and cosmetics sectors need a skilled technical workforce, entrepreneurial ecosystem, and export-ready companies. GTCI indicators most relevant here are those affecting: TVET/technical workforce (food technologists, lab analysts), new business formation, high-value exports, gender inclusion, and workforce technician density. BDFA (bdfa.gov.bn) leads halal certification and agricultural research.
sectors.wawasan_ref: Wawasan 2035 — Halal Industry Development Authority (BIBD/HIDA) | BEDB Halal Sector Plan | ASEAN Halal Standards
sectors.sg_benchmark: Singapore ranks #1 globally. Singapore's halal food export success is built on strong TVET infrastructure (rank 52 in TVET enrolment) and SME digital adoption. Brunei ranks 85th in tertiary enrolment and 56th in TVET — the skills foundation for halal industry must be strengthened.
sectors.sector_target: Develop 300 halal industry TVET-qualified technicians by 2027. Improve new business density score (currently 0/100) by submitting ROCBN data to World Bank. Grow halal food exports to BND 100M by 2030.
sectors.new_indicators: Added 1.2.3 (cluster development — Salambigar Halal Park), 5.1.1 (workforce education baseline), and 4.1.5 (formal employment quality) as directly relevant to halal industry workforce and investment conditions.

indicators.name: Vocational Enrolment
indicators.definition:
Total enrolment in upper secondary vocational and technical education (TVET) programmes as a percentage of total upper secondary enrolment. Source: UNESCO UIS ISCED Level 3 vocational data.

Official GTCI Data Source: UNESCO UIS — ISCED Level 3 Vocational Enrolment; databrowser.uis.unesco.org
indicators.why_relevant: UNESCO measures TVET enrolment (ISCED Level 3 vocational) as % of total upper secondary enrolment — directly capturing the technically trained worker pipeline for halal food, agri-business, and food processing. Food technologists, laboratory analysts, halal compliance auditors, quality assurance technicians, and agricultural technicians all enter via TVET pathways. Brunei's 20.87/100 (rank 56) means only ~1-in-5 secondary students takes a vocational track — insufficient to supply the technically skilled workforce a halal industry scale-up requires.
indicators.remarks: Score 20.87/100 (rank 56/135). Halal sector TVET actions: (1) Submit IBTE enrolment data to UNESCO UIS (uis.stats@unesco.org) as ISCED Level 3 Vocational — include all 5 IBTE campuses and private TVET providers; (2) Expand IBTE programmes for halal industry: Food Technology Diploma, Halal Quality Assurance Certificate, Agricultural Science Diploma; (3) Sign IBTE–BDFA (bdfa.gov.bn) MOU for industry-linked TVET content; (4) Target: 30% of secondary enrolment in TVET by 2028.

indicators.name: Technicians & Associate Professionals
indicators.definition:
Technicians and associate professionals (ISCO-08 Major Group 3) as a percentage of total employment. Covers science & engineering, health, business administration, legal, social, cultural, and IT technician associate professionals.

Official GTCI Data Source: ILO ILOSTAT — Employment by Occupation ISCO-08 Group 3; ilostat.ilo.org
indicators.why_relevant: ILO ISCO-08 Major Group 3 includes life science and health associate professionals (food safety analysts, lab technicians, agricultural research assistants) and business administration associates relevant to halal certification management. This is the critical mid-level talent pool for the halal food industry: food testing labs, BDFA inspection capacity, halal abattoir operations, and export certification all depend on trained technicians. Brunei's 57.06/100 (rank 36) outperforms Singapore (52.4, rank 42) here — but the challenge is channelling this technician base toward halal-specific occupations.
indicators.remarks: Score 57.06/100 (rank 36/135) — Brunei outperforms Singapore. Action: Submit LFS occupational data to ILO ILOSTAT (ilostat.ilo.org) with correct ISCO-08 coding — ensure BDFA laboratory analysts, food processing technicians, and agricultural extension officers are coded as Major Group 3 (not clerical or production workers). Expand IBTE food technology and agri-science technician programmes with BDFA (bdfa.gov.bn) as industry partner.

indicators.name: High-Value Exports
indicators.definition:
Medium-high and high-technology manufactured exports plus knowledge-intensive service exports as a percentage of total exports. Measures technological sophistication and knowledge intensity of a country's export base.

Official GTCI Data Source: UN Comtrade / World Bank WDI / OECD Technology Intensity Classification; comtrade.un.org
indicators.primary_source: UN Comtrade / World Bank WDI / OECD classification
indicators.why_relevant: UN Comtrade / OECD measures medium-high and high-technology exports as % of total exports. For the halal sector, this encompasses halal pharmaceutical manufacturing (HS chapter 30), halal cosmetics and personal care (HS 33), and processed halal food products qualifying as value-added under OECD technology intensity classification. Brunei at 1.53/100 (rank 115) reflects near-total export dependence on LNG/crude — halal food and pharmaceutical exports, while growing, are not yet classified as high-value under the OECD/UN Comtrade framework.
indicators.remarks: Score 1.53/100 (rank 115/135). Halal export actions: (1) Commission UN Comtrade classification audit for all Brunei halal exports — verify halal pharmaceutical, cosmetic, and processed food exports are correctly coded under OECD high-tech HS classifications; (2) Develop halal pharmaceutical manufacturing at PMB Industrial Zone — pharmaceutical products automatically qualify as high-tech under OECD classification; (3) Submit verified export data to UN Comtrade (comtrade.un.org) via BEDB and Ministry of Finance coordination.

indicators.name: New Business Density
indicators.definition:
New limited liability companies registered per year per 1,000 working-age adults (15–64). Measures entrepreneurial dynamism and the ease of formal business creation.

Official GTCI Data Source: World Bank Entrepreneurship Snapshots Database; worldbank.org/en/programs/entrepreneurship
indicators.why_relevant: World Bank Entrepreneurship Database measures new LLC registrations per 1,000 working-age adults — the primary indicator of SME formation dynamism. For Brunei's halal industry, new business formation is critical: halal food start-ups, SME food producers, agri-entrepreneurs, and halal cosmetics manufacturers all register through ROCBN. The score of 0/100 (rank 125) does not reflect an absence of new businesses — ROCBN registered 6,570+ enterprises in 2023 — but rather the fact that this data has never been submitted to the World Bank Entrepreneurship Database.
indicators.remarks: Score 0/100 (rank 125/135) — a pure data submission gap, highest-impact easy fix available. IMMEDIATE ACTION: ROCBN/OCP (ocp.mofe.gov.bn) to submit new enterprise registration data to World Bank Entrepreneurship Database (worldbank.org/en/programs/entrepreneurship). 2023 data (6,570 registrations) and 2024 data (~7,200 estimated) already exist. Expected score after submission: 40–60/100. This single action could lift Brunei 15–20 positions in overall GTCI ranking and signals a vibrant halal SME ecosystem to international partners.

indicators.name: Women Economic Empowerment
indicators.definition:
Laws and regulations affecting women's economic opportunities across 8 dimensions: Mobility, Workplace, Pay, Marriage, Parenthood, Entrepreneurship, Assets, and Pension. Score 0–100.

Official GTCI Data Source: World Bank Women, Business and the Law (WBL) 2024; wbl.worldbank.org
indicators.primary_source: World Bank Women, Business and the Law 2024
indicators.why_relevant: World Bank WBL measures laws affecting women's economic participation across 8 dimensions: Mobility, Workplace, Pay, Marriage, Parenthood, Entrepreneurship, Assets, and Pension. For the halal food and agri-business sector, women's economic empowerment is directly material because women constitute the majority of workers in food processing, retail, and food service, and are disproportionately represented as SME owners in the halal food sector. Legal barriers to women's economic participation directly constrain the halal industry's growth in this labour pool.
indicators.remarks: Score 53.13/100 (rank 126/135). Expert Opinion sub-score (91.3) far exceeds Legal Framework score (35.0) — practice is ahead of law. Halal sector actions: (1) Introduce equal pay provisions for halal food processing and SME employees; (2) Expand maternity leave rights in private sector to improve WBL Parenthood score; (3) Engage World Bank WBL team (wbl.worldbank.org) annually — each legislative reform directly translates to a score improvement; (4) BEDB halal sector investment pitches should feature Brunei's female entrepreneur ecosystem as a competitive asset.

indicators.name: Migrant Stock
indicators.definition:
International migrants (persons born abroad) as a percentage of total population. A proxy for openness to global talent mobility and international workforce diversity.

Official GTCI Data Source: UN Population Division — International Migrant Stock; un.org/development/desa/pd
indicators.primary_source: UN Population Division International Migrant Stock
indicators.why_relevant: UN International Migrant Stock measures international migrants as % of total population — for the halal food sector, this directly captures the availability of a diversified foreign workforce with skills not available domestically. Halal food processing, abattoir operations, plantation agriculture, and fisheries processing in Brunei rely on migrant workers (from Indonesia, Bangladesh, the Philippines). Brunei's sharp score decline (49.5/100, rank 14, -29.34 pts) reflects policy tightening that risks creating labour shortages in labour-intensive halal food production.
indicators.remarks: Score 49.5/100 (rank 14/135) — major decline of -29.34 pts. Halal sector concern: tightening work permit policies may reduce skilled halal food processing worker availability. Actions: (1) Design a Halal Industry Skills Work Pass — simplified 3-year permit for qualified halal food technologists, lab analysts, and certified halal slaughtermen; (2) Submit accurate UN Migrant Stock data to UN Population Division (un.org) annually via Immigration & National Registration Department; (3) Develop local TVET pipeline to reduce dependence on migrant workers in high-skilled halal production roles over the medium term.

indicators.why_relevant: ILO core labour standards (freedom of association, collective bargaining, non-discrimination, prohibition of forced labour) are increasingly embedded in international halal certification standards and supply chain due diligence requirements. indicators.name: Cluster Development
indicators.definition:
Measures prevalence of well-developed industry clusters — geographic concentrations of interconnected companies, suppliers, service providers and associated institutions. WEF EOS: 'How prevalent are well-developed and deep clusters?' (1=non-existent; 7=widespread). Normalised 0–100.

Official GTCI Data Source: WEF Executive Opinion Survey 2025; reports.weforum.org
indicators.why_relevant: WEF EOS measures prevalence and depth of industry clusters — geographically concentrated networks of interconnected firms, suppliers, and institutions. For the halal food sector, the Salambigar Halal Park (Brunei's dedicated halal manufacturing cluster) and Pulau Muara Besar Industrial Zone represent Brunei's primary cluster assets. The WEF score of 44.29/100 (rank 82) suggests business executives do not yet perceive Brunei's halal cluster as developed or deep — likely because its international visibility and anchor tenant count remain limited.
indicators.remarks: Score 44.29/100 (rank 82/135). Halal cluster visibility actions: (1) Brief all ~100 Brunei WEF EOS respondents annually (via BEDB, bedb.gov.bn) on Salambigar Halal Park's occupancy rates, certified producers, and export value — this directly improves the survey response; (2) Publish Annual Brunei Halal Cluster Report documenting tenant count, output value, halal certification statistics, and export destinations; (3) Attract 5 anchor halal food manufacturers (targeting OIC-country investors) to Salambigar by 2027 to deepen cluster substance and perception.

indicators.name: Workforce Secondary Education
indicators.definition:
Share of employed persons aged 15+ with at least secondary education (ISCED 2+) as a percentage of total employment. Measures educational attainment of the active workforce.

Official GTCI Data Source: ILO ILOSTAT — Educational Attainment of Employed Population; ilostat.ilo.org
indicators.why_relevant: ILO LFS measures employed persons with at least secondary education (ISCED 2+) as % of total employment — the baseline educational attainment of the halal food processing, agri-business, and food retail workforce. Secondary-educated workers are the target recruitment pool for entry-level food processing, quality inspection, and logistics roles. Brunei's 62.63/100 (rank 28) outperforms Singapore (42.1, rank 80) — the -10.6 point decline likely reflects a positive compositional shift (more tertiary graduates) rather than genuine deterioration.
indicators.remarks: Score 62.63/100 (rank 28/135). Brunei outperforms Singapore here. Action: (1) DEPS to verify that IBTE Diploma graduates are coded correctly in LFS as ISCED Level 3 completers — miscoding as below-secondary artificially suppresses this score; (2) Submit corrected LFS occupational-educational data to ILO ILOSTAT (ilostat.ilo.org) promptly after each annual LFS; (3) Develop adult secondary completion programme specifically for halal industry workers in food processing and agri-business.

indicators.name: Vulnerable Employment
indicators.definition:
Share of total employment in non-vulnerable jobs — complement of vulnerable employment (own-account workers + contributing family workers as % of total employment). Score 0–100; higher = more formal, secure employment.

Official GTCI Data Source: ILO ILOSTAT — Vulnerable Employment Rate (inverted); ilostat.ilo.org
indicators.why_relevant: ILO vulnerable employment (inverted) signals the formality and stability of the halal sector workforce. Formal employment matters for: (a) ensuring halal supply chain workers are traceable and ILO-compliant, (b) enabling BDFA food safety certification audits to verify that production workers are formally employed and trained, and (c) meeting supply chain due diligence requirements of major halal importers (UAE, Saudi Arabia, EU). Brunei's rank 10/135 is a competitive asset for positioning as a premium, supply-chain-responsible halal supplier.
indicators.remarks: Score 95.02/100 (rank 10/135) — competitive strength. Include in BEDB halal investment pitches as supply chain integrity evidence. Actions: (1) Ensure all halal food processing workers (including those at Salambigar Halal Park) are formally employed and captured in DEPS LFS data; (2) Submit comprehensive employment status data to ILO ILOSTAT (ilostat@ilo.org) annually; (3) Feature ILO formal employment rank in BDFA food safety certification marketing materials targeting premium export markets.

policies.title: New Business Density — Highest Impact Fix
policies.details: Submit ROCBN/OCP (ocp.mofe.gov.bn) new enterprise registration data to World Bank Entrepreneurship Database (worldbank.org/en/programs/entrepreneurship). This single action transforms score from 0/100. Brunei registered 6,570+ new enterprises in 2023. Data exists and only submission is missing.

policies.title: TVET for Halal Industry
policies.details: Submit IBTE enrolment data to UNESCO UIS (data.uis.unesco.org) as ISCED Level 3 Vocational. Expand IBTE food technology, halal production, and laboratory technician programmes. Partner with BDFA (bdfa.gov.bn) for industry-linked TVET content.

policies.title: High-Value Halal Exports
policies.details: Audit UN Comtrade (comtrade.un.org) classifications for halal food, pharmaceutical, and cosmetic exports. Ensure products are coded under OECD high-technology HS categories where applicable. Develop halal pharmaceutical manufacturing at PMB Industrial Zone.

policies.title: Women Economic Empowerment
policies.details: Engage World Bank Women, Business and the Law (wbl.worldbank.org) annually. Legislative reforms — equal pay provisions, expanded maternity leave, workplace harassment law — each directly improve WBL score. Halal sector employs significant numbers of women.

policies.title: Technician Workforce Development
policies.details: Submit LFS occupational data to ILO ILOSTAT (ilostat.ilo.org) with correct ISCO-08 coding for food and agriculture technicians (ISCO group 3.1.4 — life science technicians). Expand IBTE agri-food technician programmes.

policies.title: Halal Cluster Visibility
policies.details: Brief WEF EOS respondents on Salambigar Halal Park performance through BEDB (bedb.gov.bn). Publish annual halal cluster report documenting tenant count, output value, and certification statistics.

sectors.title: 💼  SECTOR 3: FINANCIAL & PROFESSIONAL SERVICES
sectors.context: Financial and professional services are central to Brunei's economic diversification. BDCB's Financial Sector Blueprint targets Brunei as an ASEAN centre for Islamic finance and fintech. GTCI indicators relevant here are those measuring: market openness, private credit depth, financial globalisation, gender inclusion in professional roles, skills availability, and new business formation. Brunei's domestic credit score (15.42/100, rank 76) reflects a structurally shallow financial market — the primary constraint on services sector growth.
sectors.wawasan_ref: Wawasan 2035 — Services Sector | BDCB Financial Sector Blueprint 2025–2035 | Brunei Islamic Finance Strategy
sectors.sg_benchmark: Singapore is the world's leading financial centre. Domestic credit score: 148.5 (vs Brunei 15.42). FDI restrictiveness: rank 1 globally (vs Brunei rank 77). The gap in financial market depth is the most important structural difference between the two economies.
sectors.sector_target: Increase domestic credit to private sector from ~38% to 55% of GDP by 2030. Improve FDI restrictiveness score by liberalising digital services and professional services sectors. Achieve Global Findex inclusion in 2027.
sectors.new_indicators: Added 6.2.6 (SME formation critical for services ecosystem), 1.1.1 and 1.1.2 (governance strengths that attract financial services FDI), 6.2.3 (fintech IP receipts), and 3.2.1 (business education for financial professionals).

indicators.name: Extent of Market Dominance
indicators.definition:
Measures the extent to which corporate activities are dominated by a few business groups. WEF EOS survey: 'In your country, how intense is competition in local markets?' (1=not intense; 7=extremely intense). Normalised 0–100.

Official GTCI Data Source: WEF Executive Opinion Survey 2025; reports.weforum.org
indicators.why_relevant: WEF EOS captures business executive perception of market competition intensity — low competition (high dominance by few firms) suppresses financial services innovation, increases cost of credit, and deters entry by fintech and professional services firms. Brunei's 39.42/100 (rank 99) reflects an economy where a small number of GLCs and established banks dominate — exactly the market structure that prevents the emergence of competitive financial services, insurance, and legal markets that Wawasan 2035's services sector diversification requires.
indicators.remarks: Score 39.42/100 (rank 99/135). Actions: (1) BDCB's Regulatory Sandbox (bdcb.gov.bn) has licensed 6 fintech firms — expand to 20 by 2027, which will demonstrably diversify the financial services market; (2) Introduce SME credit guarantee scheme to reduce bank dominance in credit allocation; (3) Brief WEF EOS respondents (BEDB pre-survey pack) on new market entrants in Islamic fintech, digital banking, and professional services; (4) Liberalise professional services licensing for foreign law firms and accounting practices in Brunei.

indicators.name: Domestic Credit to Private Sector
indicators.definition:
Financial resources provided to the private sector by financial corporations (loans, non-equity securities, trade credits) as a percentage of GDP.

Official GTCI Data Source: IMF IFS / World Bank WDI; data.worldbank.org/indicator/FS.AST.PRVT.GD.ZS
indicators.primary_source: IMF International Financial Statistics, World Bank WDI
indicators.why_relevant: IMF IFS / World Bank WDI measures private sector credit as % of GDP — the depth of the financial system's intermediation capacity, and the single most important structural indicator for financial services sector development. Without deep private credit markets, a financial services industry cannot scale. Brunei at 15.42/100 (rank 76) — private credit ~38% GDP vs Singapore's 148.5% — reflects a banking sector serving predominantly large GLCs and O&G rather than SMEs, retail borrowers, or the professional services market.
indicators.remarks: Score 15.42/100 (rank 76/135). The -20 pt drop is the most concerning deterioration in the entire GTCI. Actions: (1) BDCB to submit comprehensive monetary survey data to IMF IFS (imf.org/en/Data) — some private lending may be miscategorised; (2) Establish Brunei Credit Bureau to enable risk-based SME lending; (3) Issue annual government sukuk to develop a benchmark Islamic finance yield curve; (4) Expand BDCB Islamic credit guarantee facility for SMEs; (5) Fast-track Islamic digital banking licences to increase competition and credit availability.

indicators.name: Cluster Development
indicators.definition:
Measures prevalence of well-developed industry clusters — geographic concentrations of interconnected companies, suppliers, service providers and associated institutions. WEF EOS: 'How prevalent are well-developed and deep clusters?' (1=non-existent; 7=widespread). Normalised 0–100.

Official GTCI Data Source: WEF Executive Opinion Survey 2025; reports.weforum.org
indicators.why_relevant: WEF EOS cluster development for financial and professional services measures whether Brunei possesses a recognisable concentration of banks, insurance firms, law firms, accounting practices, and fintech companies at sufficient depth. A financial services cluster signals that the ecosystem required to support investor operations (legal, audit, financial advisory, insurance) exists locally. Brunei's 44.29/100 (rank 82) suggests EOS respondents do not perceive the financial cluster as sufficiently deep — a perception gap relative to actual capabilities.
indicators.remarks: Score 44.29/100 (rank 82/135). Actions: (1) Designate a Brunei Financial Services District in BSB — co-located banks, fintech firms, law firms, and accounting practices to deepen cluster concentration; (2) Attract 2 international law firms and 1 Big-4 accounting regional headquarters to Brunei by 2028; (3) Publish annual Brunei Financial Services Cluster Report through BDCB, giving WEF EOS respondents concrete cluster metrics; (4) Develop Islamic Finance Hub branding centred on BDCB's Brunei International Financial Centre (BIFC) initiative.

indicators.name: FDI Regulatory Restrictiveness
indicators.definition:
Measures statutory restrictions on FDI across 22 sectors. OECD index: 0=open, 1=closed; inverted in GTCI so higher score = more open to FDI.

Official GTCI Data Source: OECD FDI Regulatory Restrictiveness Index; oecd.org/investment/fdiindex.htm
indicators.why_relevant: OECD FDI Restrictiveness specifically assesses barriers to FDI in financial services — equity limits in banking and insurance, restrictions on foreign legal entities, and screening requirements for financial sector entry. Brunei's restrictions (particularly limits on foreign bank branching and insurance company ownership) are primary constraints on developing an international financial centre. The OECD index provides the authoritative annual measurement of these barriers and is directly used by investors evaluating Brunei for financial services investment.
indicators.remarks: Score 54.68/100 (rank 77/135). Financial sector FDI actions: (1) Respond to OECD FDI Restrictiveness annual questionnaire — confirm actual restrictions vs. perceived ones; (2) Allow 100% foreign equity in digital-only banks and fintech companies (no branch network required); (3) Reduce foreign equity limits in Islamic insurance (takaful) to attract Gulf Cooperation Council insurance capital; (4) Publish a Financial Sector FDI Liberalisation Roadmap jointly through BDCB and BEDB.

indicators.name: Financial Globalization
indicators.definition:
De facto and de jure financial globalisation — actual international financial flows (FDI, portfolio, income, reserves) and formal openness policies (capital account restrictions, investment agreements).

Official GTCI Data Source: KOF Globalisation Index — Financial Globalisation sub-index; kof.ethz.ch
indicators.primary_source: KOF Swiss Economic Institute Globalization Index
indicators.why_relevant: KOF measures both de facto financial integration (FDI stocks, portfolio investment, banking claims, international income) and de jure financial openness (capital account restrictions, investment agreements). For financial services, a higher KOF score signals that Brunei is well-integrated into the global financial system — attracting international capital, banks, and institutional investors. The declining score (78.93/100, rank 29, -6.54 pts) is concerning, possibly reflecting reduced international financial flows as O&G FDI slows.
indicators.remarks: Score 78.93/100 (rank 29/135). Actions: (1) BDCB to submit comprehensive BOP data to IMF (imf.org/en/Data) — including BIA (Brunei Investment Agency) sovereign wealth fund portfolio flows, which may be significantly under-reported; (2) Contact KOF Institute (kof.ethz.ch) annually before June data lock for correction requests; (3) Expand international banking partnerships — attract 2–3 additional international banks to establish Brunei branches, increasing de facto financial integration scores.

indicators.name: Household financial resilience
indicators.definition:
Measures share of adults with a bank or mobile money account AND ability to raise emergency funds — a composite of financial access and household financial resilience. Score 0–100.

Official GTCI Data Source: World Bank Global Findex Database 2024; worldbank.org/en/publication/globalfindex
indicators.why_relevant: World Bank Global Findex measures adults with a bank or mobile money account AND the ability to raise emergency funds within 30 days — the two core components of financial resilience. For financial services development, this captures depth of retail financial inclusion and household balance sheet robustness. Brunei is absent from Findex 2024 (score N/A) despite high banking penetration among nationals — a pure data submission gap that misrepresents Brunei's actual financial sector development to GTCI users and investors.
indicators.remarks: Score N/A (not in Global Findex 2024). BDCB (bdcb.gov.bn) holds national financial inclusion data showing >90% bank account ownership. IMMEDIATE ACTION: BDCB to formally request World Bank inclusion in Global Findex 2027 survey cycle (worldbank.org/en/publication/globalfindex) — timeline 12–18 months. Expected score if included: 70–85/100. This pure data gap, if resolved, dramatically improves Brunei's Retain pillar score and signals financial sector maturity to international partners.

indicators.name: Finding Skilled Employees
indicators.definition:
Measures ease of finding workers with required skills. WEF EOS: 'How easy is it for companies to find workers with the skills they require?' (1=extremely difficult; 7=extremely easy). Normalised 0–100.

Official GTCI Data Source: WEF Executive Opinion Survey 2025; reports.weforum.org
indicators.why_relevant: WEF EOS ease of finding skilled employees — for financial services, this captures whether the labour market can supply accountants, actuaries, compliance officers, financial analysts, Islamic finance structurers, and risk managers. Brunei's 42.88/100 (rank 119/135) is its worst GTCI indicator overall, and acutely relevant to financial services: international banks, insurance companies, and professional services firms will not establish Brunei operations if they cannot hire qualified local financial professionals.
indicators.remarks: Score 42.88/100 (rank 119/135) — Brunei's worst GTCI indicator. Financial services-specific actions: (1) Establish a Brunei Finance Professionals Registry with BICPA and BDCB; (2) Introduce Islamic Finance Scholar-Practitioner Programme — MOE-funded 18-month fast-track for UBD graduates; (3) Create Critical Finance Skills Work Pass for qualified CFOs, actuaries, and compliance officers — processed within 5 business days; (4) Annual National Financial Services Skills Survey to track supply–demand gaps and guide UBDSBE curriculum.

indicators.name: Education System Relevance
indicators.definition:
Measures whether the national education system meets the needs of a competitive economy. WEF EOS: 'Does the education system meet the needs of a competitive economy?' (1=not at all; 7=very well). Normalised 0–100.

Official GTCI Data Source: WEF Executive Opinion Survey 2025; reports.weforum.org
indicators.why_relevant: WEF EOS measures whether the education system produces graduates meeting the economy's needs — for financial services, this captures whether Brunei's universities produce sufficient finance professionals, accountants, economists, and data analysts with adequate practical preparation. UBD's School of Business and Economics (UBDSBE) is the primary financial services education provider. Its curriculum relevance to international financial markets and Islamic finance practice has not been formally benchmarked against industry requirements.
indicators.remarks: Score 60.78/100 (rank 26/135). Financial education actions: (1) Establish UBDSBE Industry Advisory Board (IAB) with BDCB, HSBC Brunei, and leading Islamic finance practitioners — review curriculum annually against industry needs; (2) UBDSBE to introduce CFA (Chartered Financial Analyst) Prep Programme and CIMA (Chartered Institute of Management Accountants) pathway; (3) Mandate 16-week financial sector internship for all UBDSBE graduates; (4) Brief WEF EOS respondents on UBDSBE curriculum improvements via BEDB pre-survey materials.

indicators.name: Skills Matching
indicators.definition:
Measures efficiency of the labour market in matching workers' skills to available jobs. WEF EOS: 'To what extent do the skills of the labour force match employer needs?' (1=not at all; 7=to a great extent). Normalised 0–100.

Official GTCI Data Source: WEF Executive Opinion Survey 2025; reports.weforum.org
indicators.why_relevant: WEF EOS skills matching captures efficiency of the labour market in connecting financial professionals to appropriate roles — whether JobCentre Brunei (managed by Department of Labour, Ministry of Home Affairs, labour.gov.bn) effectively places finance graduates with banks, insurance companies, and professional services firms, and whether applicant skills match what employers require. Brunei's 65.7/100 (rank 59) is moderate, with private sector placement in financial roles particularly challenging due to salary expectation gaps between public and private sector employment.
indicators.remarks: Score 65.7/100 (rank 59/135). Financial sector matching actions: (1) Department of Labour (labour.gov.bn) to develop a dedicated Financial Services Talent Matching Platform listing all open financial sector roles and required qualifications; (2) Brunei Skills Recognition Framework (BSRF) to include financial qualifications (ACCA, CPA, CFA, CIMA) as formally recognised credentials; (3) BDCB to publish annual Financial Sector Workforce Demand Report — giving employers and students visibility on emerging skill needs.

indicators.name: Gender Parity High-Skilled Jobs
indicators.definition:
Ratio of female to male employment in high-skilled occupations (ISCO-08 Major Groups 1–3: managers, professionals, technicians). Score 0–100; 100 = perfect parity.

Official GTCI Data Source: ILO ILOSTAT — Labour Force by Occupation and Sex; ilostat.ilo.org
indicators.why_relevant: ILO measures ratio of female to male employment in ISCO Major Groups 1–3 (managers, professionals, technicians). For financial services, this captures participation of women in banking management, accounting, legal, and professional advisory roles. Brunei's 82.62/100 (rank 48) reflects strong female participation in professional roles generally — women dominate education, healthcare, and government professions — but private financial services sector female C-suite representation lags behind.
indicators.remarks: Score 82.62/100 (rank 48/135). Financial services actions: (1) BDCB and Darussalam Enterprise to publish gender-disaggregated data on financial sector employment annually; (2) BEDB to track and report female-led financial services SMEs in its enterprise data; (3) Introduce 'Women in Finance' scholarship — 10 annual MOE/JPA scholarships for women pursuing CFA, actuarial, or financial analytics qualifications internationally.

indicators.name: Leadership opportunities for women
indicators.definition:
Measures extent to which leadership positions are equally open to women. WEF EOS: 'To what extent do women have equal opportunities to rise to enterprise leadership?' (1=not at all; 7=same as men). Normalised 0–100.

Official GTCI Data Source: WEF Executive Opinion Survey 2025; reports.weforum.org
indicators.why_relevant: WEF EOS measures business executives' perception of equal leadership opportunities for women — for financial services, this captures whether women can realistically reach CFO, CEO, or Board Director roles in banks, insurance companies, and professional services firms. Brunei's 71.54/100 (rank 28) reflects generally positive perception — prominent female senior civil servants and LegCo members — but private financial sector C-suite female representation lags behind these public sector gains.
indicators.remarks: Score 71.54/100 (rank 28/135). Financial services gender leadership actions: (1) BDCB to mandate that all licensed financial institutions disclose gender composition of boards and senior management in annual regulatory reports; (2) Establish a 'Brunei Women in Finance' leadership programme — annual cohort of 20 high-potential female financial professionals mentored by senior industry leaders; (3) Brief WEF EOS respondents on female leadership in financial services through annual pre-survey factsheet prepared by BEDB.

indicators.name: New Business Density
indicators.definition:
New limited liability companies registered per year per 1,000 working-age adults (15–64). Measures entrepreneurial dynamism and the ease of formal business creation.

Official GTCI Data Source: World Bank Entrepreneurship Snapshots Database; worldbank.org/en/programs/entrepreneurship
indicators.why_relevant: World Bank Entrepreneurship Database captures new LLC registrations per 1,000 working-age adults — a foundational indicator of the professional services SME ecosystem. Law firms, accounting practices, financial consultancies, and fintech start-ups all register as new businesses. The score of 0/100 (rank 125) is not because Brunei lacks new business formation, but because ROCBN data has never been submitted to the World Bank. For the financial services sector, this data gap creates an impression of entrepreneurial stagnation that deters ecosystem investment.
indicators.remarks: Score 0/100 (rank 125/135) — data submission gap. ROCBN/OCP (ocp.mofe.gov.bn) to submit new enterprise registration data to World Bank Entrepreneurship Database (worldbank.org/en/programs/entrepreneurship). This resolves Sectors 2, 3, and 5 simultaneously with a single World Bank engagement. BEDB to coordinate ROCBN and World Bank data team contact to initiate the submission — estimated effort: 2 staff-days.

indicators.name: Government Effectiveness
indicators.definition:
Perceptions of the quality of public services, the quality of the civil service and the degree of its independence from political pressures, the quality of policy formulation and implementation, and the credibility of the government's commitment to such policies. Score 0–100; higher = more effective.

Official GTCI Data Source: World Bank WGI — Government Effectiveness Estimate; data.worldbank.org/indicator/GE.EST
indicators.primary_source: World Bank, The Worldwide Governance Indicators
indicators.why_relevant: For financial services, WGI Government Effectiveness specifically signals the reliability of financial regulatory authorities — BDCB's supervisory capacity, consistency of banking licence approval processes, and quality of monetary policy implementation. International financial institutions and fund managers assess regulatory effectiveness before establishing Brunei operations. Brunei's rank 17/135 is a comparative advantage over most ASEAN peers in signalling predictable, professional financial regulation.
indicators.remarks: Score 80.06/100 (rank 17/135). Financial regulatory actions: (1) BDCB to publish annual Regulatory Performance Report — response times for licence approvals, enforcement actions, and regulatory consultations; (2) Highlight BDCB's Financial Sector Blueprint 2025–2035 in WEF EOS pre-survey materials as evidence of structured regulatory governance; (3) Submit BDCB regulatory data to IMF Financial Sector Assessment Programme (FSAP) to demonstrate regulatory quality internationally.

indicators.name: Rule of Law
indicators.definition:
Captures perceptions of the extent to which agents have confidence in and abide by the rules of society — in particular contract enforcement, property rights, police, courts — as well as the likelihood of crime and violence. Score 0–100; higher = stronger rule of law.

Official GTCI Data Source: World Bank WGI — Rule of Law Estimate; data.worldbank.org/indicator/RL.EST
indicators.why_relevant: WGI Rule of Law for financial services measures quality of contract enforcement critical for financial transactions: bond indentures, loan agreements, insurance claims, and securities contracts must be enforceable with certainty and speed. Brunei's 73.77/100 (rank 29) is a moderate advantage — English common law system, functioning courts — but the absence of a dedicated commercial arbitration centre and slow commercial dispute resolution are weaknesses noted by international financial institutions considering Brunei operations.
indicators.remarks: Score 73.77/100 (rank 29/135). Financial sector rule-of-law actions: (1) Establish Brunei International Financial Arbitration Centre (BIFAC) — low-cost, fast-track arbitration targeting 90-day resolution for commercial and financial disputes; (2) Implement electronic court filing for commercial cases; (3) Submit judiciary commercial performance data to World Bank surveys — feeding indirectly into WGI Rule of Law scores.

indicators.name: Intellectual Property Receipts
indicators.definition:
Charges for the use of intellectual property received from abroad (royalties, patent licensing, franchise fees) as a percentage of total trade. Measures capacity to generate commercially valuable IP and license it internationally.

Official GTCI Data Source: IMF Balance of Payments Statistics / WTO Trade Database; imf.org/en/Data

indicators.why_relevant: IMF BOP IP receipts — for financial services, this could arise from Islamic finance product IP (sukuk structuring patents), fintech software licensing, and financial data analytics licensing. Brunei at 0/100 earns no IP income currently. The aspiration to be an Islamic finance hub creates opportunity: proprietary sharia-compliant financial product structures, fintech compliance software, and Islamic finance certification standards could all generate international licensing income and position Brunei as an Islamic finance IP exporter.
indicators.remarks: Score 0/100 (rank 121/135). Fintech and Islamic finance IP actions: (1) BruIPO (bruipo.gov.bn) to develop Islamic finance IP filing programme — patent support for sharia-compliant fintech innovations from BDCB Regulatory Sandbox graduates; (2) Introduce Patent Box tax regime for IP income from financial technology and Islamic finance innovations; (3) BDCB Regulatory Sandbox graduates to be required to register novel financial product structures as intellectual property before commercial launch.

indicators.name: Business Masters Education
indicators.definition:
Quality of national business schools as measured by QS Global MBA Rankings and Financial Times Global MBA Rankings. Score 0–100 based on rank of best national business school.

Official GTCI Data Source: QS Global MBA Rankings 2025; FT Business Education Rankings; topmba.com
indicators.primary_source: QS Global MBA and Business Masters Rankings 2025
indicators.why_relevant: QS/FT MBA rankings measure quality of business school education — for financial services, a world-class business school is infrastructure that financial centres must possess. Singapore's NUS Business School and SMU anchor the financial services ecosystem by producing top-tier finance talent globally. Brunei's UBDSBE has no international ranking (score 0/100), limiting its ability to develop the high-calibre financial professionals, corporate finance advisors, and investment managers that an aspiring financial centre requires.
indicators.remarks: Score 0/100 (rank 64/135). Business education actions: (1) UBDSBE to submit AACSB Eligibility Application (accreditation@aacsb.edu) — prerequisite for QS/FT ranking eligibility; budget BND 150,000–200,000; (2) Negotiate joint MBA with NUS Business School or Warwick Business School — co-badging accelerates ranking timeline; (3) Launch UBD Islamic Finance MBA — 12-month programme combining Islamic jurisprudence, fintech, and conventional finance; target CIMB, Baiduri, and Bank Islam Brunei as corporate sponsors.

policies.title: Financial Market Deepening
policies.details: BDCB (bdcb.gov.bn) to submit comprehensive monetary survey data to IMF IFS (imf.org/en/Data). Develop national credit bureau to enable private sector lending. Expand BDCB Regulatory Sandbox for fintech. Issue annual government sukuk to develop benchmark yield curve.

policies.title: FDI Liberalisation in Services
policies.details: Submit annual OECD FDI Restrictiveness questionnaire (oecd.org/investment/fdiindex.htm). Reduce restrictions in digital services (allow 100% foreign ownership in fintech) and professional services sectors. Publish FDI liberalisation roadmap.

policies.title: Financial Globalisation
policies.details: Submit BIA portfolio and FDI flow data to IMF BOP (imf.org/en/Data) comprehensively. Contact KOF Institute (kof.ethz.ch) for annual data verification. Expand international banking presence in Brunei.

policies.title: Global Findex Inclusion
policies.details: BDCB to request Brunei inclusion in World Bank Global Findex 2027 (worldbank.org/en/publication/globalfindex). Brunei's actual financial inclusion exceeds 90% among nationals. Submit BDCB financial inclusion statistics as evidence of data capacity.

policies.title: Skills for Financial Services
policies.details: Submit LFS educational attainment data to ILO ILOSTAT (ilostat.ilo.org). Expand UBD Finance and Accounting programmes. Engage WEF EOS panel through BEDB (bedb.gov.bn) to brief respondents on financial sector workforce development.

policies.title: Women in Financial Services
policies.details: Engage World Bank WBL team (wbl.worldbank.org) on legislative reforms. Financial services sector requires legislative equal pay and workplace protection to attract female professional talent. Each WBL reform directly improves score.

intl_orgs.name: IMF Data
intl_orgs.role: BDCB (bdcb.gov.bn) submits comprehensive BOP, monetary survey, and financial statistics. Feeds domestic credit (1.2.2), financial globalisation (2.1.2), and IP receipts (6.2.3).

intl_orgs.role: Annual FDI Restrictiveness questionnaire response. Direct impact on 2.1.1 FDI openness score.

intl_orgs.name: World Bank Global Findex
intl_orgs.role: Request Brunei inclusion in Findex 2027. BDCB financial inclusion data supports a 70–85/100 expected score.

intl_orgs.name: KOF Institute
intl_orgs.role: Annual data correction requests before June lock. Feeds 2.1.2 financial globalisation score.

intl_orgs.name: World Bank WBL

intl_orgs.name: World Bank Enterprise Surveys
intl_orgs.role: Request WBES inclusion for Brunei. Feeds 1.3.7 and 3.2.2 — both currently zero. One request fixes both.

sectors.title: 🌺  SECTOR 4: TOURISM, HOSPITALITY & CULTURAL ECONOMY
sectors.context: Tourism is a priority sector under Wawasan 2035 with targets to increase visitor arrivals and tourism receipts. Brunei's strengths — political stability, safety, sanitation, and low crime — are world-class but poorly captured in GTCI data because key sources (Social Progress Index, World Risk Poll, Gallup) do not survey Brunei. Several tourism-critical indicators currently score zero purely because of data submission gaps, not actual performance. These represent the highest-potential unrealised GTCI gains available to Brunei in any single sector.
sectors.wawasan_ref: Wawasan 2035 — Tourism Sector | Brunei Tourism Master Plan | Tourism Development Department / TDD (tourism.gov.bn) | ASEAN Tourism Agreement
sectors.sg_benchmark: Singapore ranks #1 globally. Singapore's tourism competitiveness is built on personal safety (rank 4), quality lifestyle (top-tier SPI scores), and strong talent retention. Brunei's actual safety and sanitation performance matches or exceeds Singapore but is invisible in GTCI data.
sectors.sector_target: Submit RBPF crime data to UNODC (unodc.org/unodc/en/data-and-analysis) to establish Brunei in Personal Safety benchmarks. Request Social Progress Imperative inclusion. Target combined Retain-Lifestyle pillar improvement of 10 points by 2027.
sectors.new_indicators: Added 4.1.1 (pension — worker quality signal), 4.1.4 (environmental performance — eco-tourism), and 2.2.3 (social mobility — welcoming society) as directly relevant to tourism competitiveness and destination brand quality.

indicators.name: Personal rights
indicators.definition:
Composite measure of personal and political rights including political rights, freedom of speech and assembly, and freedom of movement. Score 0–100; higher = more rights protected.

Official GTCI Data Source: Social Progress Imperative — Social Progress Index (SPI), Personal Rights sub-index; socialprogress.org
indicators.why_relevant: Social Progress Imperative measures personal and political rights — for tourism, freedom of movement, rights of foreign visitors, and absence of arbitrary restrictions on tourist activities are assessed. While Brunei's MIB constitutional framework creates certain behavioural restrictions (alcohol, dress codes), actual personal safety and freedom of movement for tourists are generally well-protected. The SPI score of N/A reflects Brunei's absence from the survey — not necessarily poor performance across all rights dimensions.
indicators.remarks: Score N/A (not in Social Progress Index). Tourism actions: (1) Engage Social Progress Imperative (socialprogress.org) to include Brunei in the SPI — submit governance and rights framework documentation; (2) Tourism Development Department / TDD (tourism.gov.bn) marketing to proactively address the alcohol prohibition in tourism communications, framing Brunei's halal-friendly environment as a positive differentiator for Muslim tourist markets; (3) Expected SPI Personal Rights score if included: 40–55/100, reflecting specific regulatory restrictions alongside generally safe and free movement.

indicators.name: Personal safety
indicators.definition:
Composite measure of personal physical safety covering homicide rate, violent crime, perceived criminality, political terror, and traffic deaths. Score 0–100; higher = safer.

Official GTCI Data Source: Social Progress Imperative — SPI, Personal Safety sub-index; socialprogress.org
indicators.why_relevant: Social Progress Imperative measures personal safety via a composite of homicide rate, violent crime, perceived criminality, political terror, and traffic deaths — the most directly tourism-relevant lifestyle indicator in the GTCI. Tourists choose destinations on safety, and travel advisories directly impact visitor arrivals. Brunei's actual crime rate is among Southeast Asia's lowest — near-zero homicide, minimal street crime — but this is invisible in GTCI because Brunei is absent from the SPI. The full gap (0 vs Singapore 93.1) is entirely a data submission issue.
indicators.remarks: Score N/A — highest unrealised tourism indicator. IMMEDIATE ACTIONS: (1) RBPF (rbpf.gov.bn) to submit annual crime statistics to UNODC Crime Statistics Database (unodc.org) — homicide rate, robbery rate, assault rate — before December annually; (2) Submit road traffic fatality data to WHO Global Road Safety (who.int/data/gho) — Brunei's data is absent from the global database; (3) Armed with UNODC data, formally request Social Progress Imperative (socialprogress.org) inclusion — expected score: 70–85/100 based on actual safety record.

indicators.name: Physician density
indicators.definition:
Number of physicians (medical doctors) per 1,000 population. Key measure of healthcare access and quality of the medical system.

Official GTCI Data Source: WHO National Health Workforce Accounts (NHWA) / Global Health Observatory; apps.who.int/gho/data
indicators.why_relevant: WHO NHWA measures physicians per 1,000 population — for tourism, physician density signals healthcare infrastructure quality available to visitors requiring medical attention. Medical tourism is also an emerging sub-sector: RIPAS Hospital and JPMC, supported by PAPRSB-IHS-trained doctors, aspire to attract medical tourism from Indonesia and the Philippines. Brunei at 27.37/100 (rank 77) shows 1.87 physicians per 1,000 population — below OECD standards but above ASEAN average, with heavy reliance on expatriate medical professionals.
indicators.remarks: Score 27.37/100 (rank 77/135). Medical tourism actions: (1) MOH to submit comprehensive physician data to WHO NHWA (apps.who.int/gho/data) by 30 September annually — include all private sector and expatriate physicians practicing in Brunei; (2) Expand PAPRSB-IHS (phs.edu.bn) medical graduate intake by 20 additional students/year; (3) Develop Brunei Medical Tourism product — JPMC as regional centre for Islamic-compliant elective procedures targeting Indonesian Muslim patients.

indicators.name: Sanitation
indicators.definition:
Percentage of population using at least basic sanitation services — improved facilities not shared with other households. Score 0–100.

Official GTCI Data Source: WHO/UNICEF Joint Monitoring Programme (JMP) for Water Supply, Sanitation and Hygiene; washdata.org
indicators.why_relevant: WHO/UNICEF JMP measures basic sanitation coverage as % of population using improved facilities not shared with other households. For tourism, sanitation quality directly affects visitor experience and health safety. Tourists from advanced economies have high sanitation expectations. Brunei's 98/100 (rank 43) near-universal sanitation coverage is a world-class infrastructure achievement that directly supports premium tourism positioning and should be a central element of destination marketing narratives.
indicators.remarks: Score 98/100 (rank 43/135) — top 5% globally. Tourism brand application: (1) Submit annual sanitation coverage data to WHO/UNICEF JMP (washdata.org) — maintain this excellent score; (2) Feature sanitation rank prominently in Tourism Development Department (tourism.gov.bn) marketing: 'Brunei ranks among the world's top 45 countries for sanitation and clean water'; (3) Combine with Political Stability rank 2 in 'Safe Clean Destination' brand campaign — a differentiated positioning in ASEAN's competitive tourism market.

indicators.name: Employee Wellbeing
indicators.definition:
Gallup World Poll Life Ladder (Cantril Self-Anchoring Scale) — subjective wellbeing on 0–10 scale (0=worst possible life; 10=best possible life). Used as proxy for employee happiness and workplace wellbeing.

Official GTCI Data Source: Gallup World Poll — Life Ladder (Happiness) Score; gallup.com
indicators.why_relevant: Gallup World Poll Life Ladder (Cantril Scale) captures overall life satisfaction — a proxy for workforce motivation, service quality, and the social atmosphere encountered by tourists. Tourism service quality is fundamentally a human factor: contented service workers create positive visitor experiences. Brunei is absent from the Gallup survey (N/A), but based on welfare indicators (free healthcare, subsidised housing, low crime, universal pension), Brunei's life satisfaction is likely moderate-to-high and would support positive tourism narratives.
indicators.remarks: Score N/A (not in Gallup World Poll). Actions: (1) DEPS (deps.mofe.gov.bn) to commission annual National Wellbeing Survey using Gallup-compatible Life Ladder methodology (~BND 50,000–100,000); (2) Request Gallup World Poll (gallup.com/analytics) to include Brunei in rolling annual survey; (3) Tourism industry employee wellbeing data to be collected by Tourism Development Department (TDD) as part of annual tourism workforce survey; (4) Expected Gallup Life Ladder score if Brunei were surveyed: 5.5–6.5/10 (moderate-high; comparable to Malaysia ~5.7).

indicators.name: Brain Gain
indicators.definition:
Measures the capacity of a country to attract highly skilled foreign talent. WEF EOS: 'Does your country attract talented people from abroad?' (1=not at all; 7=to a great extent). Normalised 0–100.

Official GTCI Data Source: WEF Executive Opinion Survey 2025; reports.weforum.org
indicators.why_relevant: WEF EOS Brain Gain measures perceived talent attraction capacity — for tourism, this captures whether Brunei can attract skilled hospitality professionals, hotel managers, chefs, and tourism practitioners from abroad. Brunei's rank 16/135 globally means it is perceived as a desirable destination for international workers — the same factors driving professional migration (safety, quality of life, income stability, political stability) substantially overlap with tourism attractiveness factors.
indicators.remarks: Score 66.32/100 (rank 16/135) — competitive strength. Tourism applications: (1) Leverage rank 16 Brain Gain in tourism promotion: a country perceived as attractive to global talent is also welcoming to visitors; (2) Brief WEF EOS respondents on Brunei's March 2025 5-year multi-entry Business Visa as a concrete talent and tourism openness signal; (3) Tourism Development Department (TDD) to feature internationally recruited hospitality professionals in Brunei hotels as evidence of a globally-connected, professional tourism workforce.

indicators.name: Tolerance of minorities
indicators.definition:
Measures minority tolerance and absence of group grievances — the degree to which identity groups coexist without institutionalised exclusion. Score 0–100; higher = more tolerant.

Official GTCI Data Source: Fund for Peace Fragile States Index — Group Grievances sub-indicator; fragilestatesindex.org
indicators.primary_source: The Fund for Peace. Fragile States Index 2024
indicators.why_relevant: Fund for Peace FSI measures group grievances and minority tolerance — for tourism, tolerance is a destination attractiveness factor for tourists from diverse religious, ethnic, and cultural backgrounds. Indonesia, Malaysia, and the Philippines — Brunei's primary tourist source markets — are diverse societies whose citizens are sensitive to reports of discriminatory treatment. Brunei's 27.66/100 (rank 93) is largely driven by the FSI's assessment of the MIB constitutional framework and treatment of non-Muslim communities.
indicators.remarks: Score 27.66/100 (rank 93/135). Tourism context: Brunei's actual tourist experience for non-Muslim visitors is widely reported as respectful and culturally engaging. Actions: (1) Submit annual inter-faith dialogue and cultural events documentation to Fund for Peace (fragilestatesindex.org) before April data lock — Chinese New Year, Deepavali, and Christian community activities in official reports; (2) Tourism Development Department (TDD) to promote Brunei's cultural diversity through 'Cultural Heritage' tourism products featuring Chinese, Malay, and indigenous Iban communities; (3) Train tourism frontline workers in inclusive service protocols for international visitors.
