indicators.name: Brain Retention
indicators.definition:
Measures the extent to which a country retains talent rather than losing it to emigration. WEF EOS: 'Does your country retain talented people?' (1=not at all; 7=to a great extent). Normalised 0–100.

Official GTCI Data Source: WEF Executive Opinion Survey 2025; reports.weforum.org
indicators.why_relevant: WEF EOS Brain Retention measures extent to which talent chooses to remain in the country — for tourism, brain retention correlates with quality-of-life conditions that also make Brunei an attractive tourist destination. A country that retains its educated professionals is one where the work-life environment is sufficiently rewarding — signalling quality restaurants, arts, culture, recreation, and social connectivity that equally benefit visitors. Brunei's improving 48.17/100 (rank 61) reflects an emerging retention dynamic as lifestyle and economic conditions improve.
indicators.remarks: Score 48.17/100 (rank 61/135). Tourism-retention linkage: (1) Develop Brunei 'Returnee Entrepreneur' programme — Bruneian professionals returning from overseas can open tourism-related businesses (boutique hotels, eco-lodges, culinary tourism operators); (2) Brief WEF EOS respondents on Brunei's improving talent retention through BEDB's pre-survey communications; (3) MOH mental health and work-life balance programmes for hospitality sector workers — addressing a sector-specific retention challenge.

indicators.name: Vulnerable Employment
indicators.definition:
Share of total employment in non-vulnerable jobs — complement of vulnerable employment (own-account workers + contributing family workers as % of total employment). Score 0–100; higher = more formal, secure employment.

Official GTCI Data Source: ILO ILOSTAT — Vulnerable Employment Rate (inverted); ilostat.ilo.org
indicators.why_relevant: ILO formal employment measure (inverted vulnerable employment rate) — for tourism, formal employment signals that Brunei's hospitality workforce operates under proper contracts with social protection, rather than precarious informal arrangements. International hotel brands (Marriott, IHG, Rizqun International) require local workforce partners to meet ILO employment standards in their global supply chain policies. Brunei's rank 10/135 globally is a strong competitive signal in this regard.
indicators.remarks: Score 95.02/100 (rank 10/135). Tourism workforce actions: (1) Ensure all hospitality sector workers — including those employed through labour supply agencies at hotels and resorts — are captured in formal employment data submitted to ILO ILOSTAT; (2) Feature Brunei's formal employment standard in Tourism Development Department (TDD) and BEDB tourism investment pitches — distinguishes Brunei from regional competitors where tourism worker social protection is weaker; (3) Publish annual Tourism Development Department Annual Tourism Workforce Report documenting employment conditions in the sector.

indicators.name: Protect against future disasters
indicators.definition:
Measures the degree to which people feel protected against future economic, climate, and security disasters. Based on perceived ability to cope with future shocks at individual and community level.

Official GTCI Data Source: Lloyd's Register Foundation World Risk Poll 2024; wrp.lrfoundation.org.uk
indicators.why_relevant: Lloyd's Register Foundation World Risk Poll measures perceived individual and community resilience against future economic, climate, and security shocks. Post-COVID, tourists and travel insurers increasingly assess destinations' ability to manage future pandemics, natural disasters, and climate risks. Brunei (N/A) is absent from the WRP, but Brunei's actual disaster preparedness (NDMC, flood infrastructure, COVID response record) is strong — another invisible asset that would score well if measured.
indicators.remarks: Score N/A (not in World Risk Poll 2024). Tourism resilience actions: (1) Engage Lloyd's Register Foundation (wrp.lrfoundation.org.uk) for Brunei inclusion in WRP 2027; (2) Submit DOSH (labour.gov.bn) workplace safety statistics as supporting evidence; (3) Publish Brunei National Disaster Risk Management Framework publicly on NDMC website; (4) Tourism Development Department (TDD) to develop a 'Safe Travel Protocol' certification for Brunei tourism operators — evidence-based safety assurance for international tourists.

indicators.name: Pension Coverage
indicators.definition:
Percentage of the working-age population covered by a pension scheme (contributory or non-contributory). Score 0–100; 100 = universal coverage.

Official GTCI Data Source: ILO Social Security Inquiry / UN SDG Database 1.3.1; ilostat.ilo.org
indicators.primary_source: International Labour Organization Social Security Inquiry
indicators.why_relevant: ILO Social Security Inquiry measures pension coverage as % of employed persons. For tourism sector workers — typically lower-wage and service-industry — pension coverage signals genuine employment security and employer social responsibility. Brunei's 100/100 (rank 1 globally) means every employed tourism worker is mandatorily covered by TAP (Tabung Amanah Pekerja). This is a unique competitive advantage in ASEAN's tourism labour market and a distinguishing factor for international hotel brand investment in Brunei.
indicators.remarks: Score 100/100 (rank 1/135) — world's best performer. Tourism marketing application: (1) Tourism Development Department (TDD) to feature in international hotel investment pitches: 'Brunei: where every tourism worker has pension protection — global rank 1'; (2) Feature universal pension coverage in BEDB tourism sector FDI materials — distinguishes Brunei from Indonesia, the Philippines, and Vietnam where tourism worker social protection is far weaker; (3) TAP (tap.com.bn) to submit annual coverage data to ILO Social Security Inquiry to maintain rank 1.

indicators.name: Environmental Performance
indicators.definition:
Composite score across 40 environmental performance indicators in 11 issue categories covering ecosystem vitality and environmental health (air quality, water, biodiversity, forests, fisheries, climate, energy, waste, agriculture). Score 0–100.

Official GTCI Data Source: Yale Center for Environmental Law & Policy — Environmental Performance Index (EPI) 2024; epi.yale.edu
indicators.primary_source: Yale Center for Environmental Law & Policy EPI 2024
indicators.why_relevant: Yale EPI measures environmental quality across 40 indicators in 11 categories — for tourism, environmental quality is the product itself: Brunei's 70%+ forest coverage, UNESCO Biosphere Reserve (Tasek Merimbun), marine biodiversity (Brunei Bay), and low pollution in interior areas are the natural capital that eco-tourism, adventure tourism, and nature tourism monetise. Brunei's 48.3/100 (rank 55) is constrained by O&G sector carbon intensity, but the actual natural environment quality is world-class in biodiversity and forest dimensions.
indicators.remarks: Score 48.3/100 (rank 55/135). Eco-tourism and EPI actions: (1) MOE (Ministry of Development environment dept) to submit annual environmental data to Yale EPI (epi.yale.edu) before January data lock; (2) Submit forest coverage data to Global Forest Watch (globalforestwatch.org) — feeds into EPI Biodiversity & Habitat sub-scores; (3) Develop Brunei UNESCO Biosphere Reserve (Tasek Merimbun) as flagship eco-tourism product with verified conservation data; (4) Tourism Development Department (TDD) to develop 'Rainforest Brunei' brand campaign leveraging EPI-verified environmental data.

indicators.name: Social Mobility
indicators.definition:
Perceived equality of opportunity for economic and social advancement. WEF EOS: 'To what extent can people with low incomes improve their economic status?' (1=very little; 7=a great extent). Normalised 0–100.

Official GTCI Data Source: WEF Executive Opinion Survey 2025; reports.weforum.org
indicators.why_relevant: WEF EOS measures perceived social mobility — the extent to which socioeconomic background does not determine economic outcomes. For tourism, a socially mobile society signals an egalitarian, welcoming culture in which all community members participate in and benefit from tourism development. Brunei's improving 60.68/100 (rank 52, +24.32 pts) reflects the positive impact of universal education scholarships (MKMA, JPA), subsidised housing, and welfare provision in creating upward mobility — a genuine and marketable destination narrative.
indicators.remarks: Score 60.68/100 (rank 52/135) — improving strongly. Tourism brand application: (1) Feature Brunei's social mobility improvements in Tourism Development Department (TDD)'s 'Authentic Brunei' destination narrative — a society where fishing village children become doctors through government scholarships tells an aspirational story resonating with ASEAN tourists; (2) Leverage Kampong Ayer (water village) community development as a tourism product visibly demonstrating social mobility in action; (3) Brief WEF EOS respondents on Brunei's universal scholarship programmes as concrete social mobility evidence.

sectors.title: 💻  SECTOR 5: ICT, DIGITAL ECONOMY & CREATIVE INDUSTRIES
sectors.context: ICT is Brunei's most strategically ambitious diversification sector. The Digital Economy Masterplan 2025 (DE25) 2025–2030 targets BND 3.3 billion in digital economy contribution. GTCI's ICT-related indicators span infrastructure, enterprise adoption, skills, exports, and innovation output. Brunei's strongest GTCI indicator is digital skills (6.1.6 — rank 1 globally, score 100/100 based on ITU IDI infrastructure proxy). However, actual enterprise software adoption (rank 97), cloud computing (rank 122), ICT exports (rank 112), and AI talent (not measured) reveal that connectivity has not yet translated into economic digital activity.
sectors.wawasan_ref: Wawasan 2035 — ICT Sector | Digital Economy Masterplan 2025 (DE25) 2025–2030 | AITI (aiti.gov.bn) | Digital Brunei National Strategy
sectors.sg_benchmark: Singapore ranks #1 globally in GTCI 2025. ICT exports: rank 3 globally (82.4/100 vs Brunei 2.44). Enterprise software: rank 4 (64.2 vs Brunei 11.86). AI talent: rank 3. The challenge for Brunei is converting world-class connectivity (rank 1 in digital skills) into economic digital output.
sectors.sector_target: Improve ICT services exports score from 2.44 to 15/100 by 2030. Increase enterprise software adoption from rank 97 to rank 70. Achieve AI talent coverage in LinkedIn Economic Graph by 2027.
sectors.new_indicators: Added 5.2.1 (ICT skills gap — most critical constraint on AITI targets), 6.1.4 (research capacity for ICT innovation), 1.2.4 (R&D investment for digital sector), and 6.2.7 (ICT research publications) as directly limiting factors on ICT sector growth.

indicators.name: Population covered by at least a 3G Mobile Network
indicators.definition:
Percentage of the population covered by a mobile network of at least 3G technology. Measures geographic and demographic reach of mobile broadband infrastructure.

Official GTCI Data Source: ITU DataHub; datahub.itu.int
indicators.why_relevant: ITU IDI measures population covered by mobile broadband (3G+) — the foundational infrastructure layer for all digital economy activity. For Brunei's ICT sector, network coverage determines the addressable market for digital services, mobile commerce, and app-based platforms. Brunei's 93.09/100 (rank 64) with 4G/LTE covering 96%+ of population and 5G launched in 2023 (DST, Progresif) provides a strong infrastructure base. The gap to Singapore (99.0) is only 5.9 pts — primarily in rural Temburong coverage.
indicators.remarks: Score 93.09/100 (rank 64/135). Actions: (1) AITI (aiti.gov.bn) to submit annual network coverage data to ITU (itustats@itu.int) before January deadline — include 5G coverage statistics; (2) Extend 5G coverage to Temburong and interior Belait by 2026 — closes the rural coverage gap and improves ITU IDI score; (3) Publish annual Brunei ICT Infrastructure Report through AITI — providing WEF EOS respondents with current data for related indicators (1.2.6, 1.3.5, 1.3.6).

indicators.name: Internet access in schools
indicators.definition:
Business executive perception of the quality and availability of internet access in schools. WEF EOS: 'How do you assess the quality of internet access in schools?' (1=very poor; 7=excellent). Normalised 0–100.

Official GTCI Data Source: WEF Executive Opinion Survey 2025 / UNESCO UIS ICT in Education Survey; reports.weforum.org
indicators.why_relevant: WEF EOS measures business executive perception of internet access quality in schools — critical for the ICT sector's talent pipeline. School internet connectivity determines the digital literacy of future ICT workers, developers, and digital entrepreneurs. Brunei's N/A score reflects a data submission failure, not poor infrastructure: OGN (One Government Network, managed by EGNC — egnc.gov.bn) connects all government schools with broadband. The ICT sector directly suffers from this misrepresentation, which distorts the perception of Brunei's digital education pipeline to international investors.
indicators.remarks: Score N/A — highest-impact data submission gap for the ICT sector. Immediate actions: (1) MOE ICT Division to complete UNESCO UIS Annual Education Survey Section 4 (ICT in Education) by 31 March annually — uis.stats@unesco.org; (2) AITI to include school connectivity data in ITU annual questionnaire (itustats@itu.int) — October deadline; (3) MOE to prepare 1-page 'Brunei School Digital Infrastructure Factsheet' for WEF EOS respondents via BEDB pre-survey pack; (4) Expected score when submitted: 75–85/100.

indicators.name: Enterprise Software
indicators.definition:
Market size for enterprise software applications (ERP, CRM, SCM, BI tools) expressed as per-capita USD revenue. Measures depth of business digitalisation across the private sector.

Official GTCI Data Source: Statista Technology Market Outlook — Enterprise Software; statista.com/outlook/tmo/software/enterprise-software
indicators.primary_source: Statista Technology Market Outlook, World Bank WDI
indicators.why_relevant: Statista measures enterprise software market per-capita revenue (ERP, CRM, SCM, BI tools) — for the ICT sector, enterprise software adoption drives demand for ICT professionals, systems integrators, software developers, and digital consultants. Low adoption (Brunei 11.86/100, rank 97) means a shallow domestic ICT services market. Building a digital economy requires firms to be sophisticated buyers and users of software — not just connectivity consumers — creating the demand that sustains ICT sector employment and revenue.
indicators.remarks: Score 11.86/100 (rank 97/135). Digital adoption actions: (1) Darussalam Enterprise (dare.gov.bn) to introduce Digital Transformation Grants — BND 10,000–50,000 per SME for ERP/CRM/cloud adoption — directly increasing enterprise software market size captured by Statista; (2) Mandate ERP adoption for all government-linked companies (GLCs) by 2027; (3) Digital Economy Masterplan 2025 (DE25) to include enterprise software adoption targets by sector — manufacturing, halal, tourism, professional services — with annual progress tracking.

indicators.name: Cloud Computing
indicators.definition:
Market size for public cloud services (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) expressed as per-capita USD revenue. Measures adoption of cloud-based digital infrastructure by businesses and government.

Official GTCI Data Source: Statista Technology Market Outlook — Public Cloud; statista.com/outlook/tmo/public-cloud
indicators.primary_source: Statista Technology Market Outlook, World Bank WDI
indicators.why_relevant: Statista measures public cloud services per-capita revenue (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) — cloud adoption is the defining metric of digital economy maturity. Brunei's 4.79/100 (rank 122) reflects minimal cloud adoption despite excellent 4G/5G infrastructure — the infrastructure exists but businesses and government have not migrated workloads to cloud. Cloud adoption generates demand for cloud architects, DevOps engineers, and cybersecurity professionals — the high-value knowledge economy jobs that Wawasan 2035 targets.
indicators.remarks: Score 4.79/100 (rank 122/135). Cloud economy actions: (1) Government Chief Information Officer to issue Cloud-First Policy — all new government IT systems to default to approved cloud platforms; (2) Migrate non-sensitive government workloads to cloud by 2027 — this alone substantially increases Brunei's cloud market size captured by Statista; (3) AITI to develop Sovereign Cloud Framework defining data classification rules for cloud migration; (4) SME Cloud Adoption Voucher (BND 5,000/company) through Darussalam Enterprise — target 500 SMEs by 2027.

indicators.name: Firms with website
indicators.definition:
Percentage of firms that use a website to conduct business, market products, or communicate with customers/suppliers. Measures digital business integration at enterprise level.

Official GTCI Data Source: World Bank Enterprise Surveys (WBES); enterprisesurveys.org
indicators.why_relevant: World Bank Enterprise Surveys measures percentage of firms with a website — a baseline measure of digital business integration. For Brunei's ICT sector, firm website penetration is the foundation of the digital commerce ecosystem: without businesses having web presences, there is no sustainable digital marketing industry, e-commerce, or digital advertising market. Brunei's 0/100 is a survey exclusion gap — WBES last covered Brunei in 2015 — not a reflection of actual business digitalisation levels.
indicators.remarks: Score 0/100 (not in WBES since 2015). Actions: (1) Ministry of Finance & Economy to formally request World Bank WBES team (enterprisesurveys@worldbank.org) to include Brunei in the 2025/2026 survey cycle — one letter simultaneously fixes 1.3.7 and 3.2.2; (2) Darussalam Enterprise to conduct national Business Digitalisation Census capturing website ownership, social media presence, and e-commerce adoption; (3) AITI to track 'Firms with Web Presence' as a KPI in the Digital Economy Masterplan dashboard.

indicators.name: AI skills migration
indicators.definition:
Net AI skills migration — the difference between inflow and outflow of workers with AI-related skills, measured by LinkedIn profile data. Positive = net attractor of AI talent.

Official GTCI Data Source: LinkedIn Economic Graph — AI Skills Migration Index; economicgraph.linkedin.com
indicators.why_relevant: LinkedIn Economic Graph measures net AI skills migration — whether a country is a net attractor or exporter of AI-skilled talent. For Brunei's ICT sector, attracting AI professionals is central to AITI's ambition to build domestic AI capability. An N/A score means Brunei is invisible in LinkedIn's global AI talent tracking — neither attracting nor repelling AI talent in a measurable way. LinkedIn visibility is a prerequisite for the ICT sector to participate meaningfully in global AI talent competition.
indicators.remarks: Score N/A (Brunei not in LinkedIn Economic Graph AI dataset). Actions: (1) MTIC/AITI (aiti.gov.bn) to engage LinkedIn Economic Graph team (economicgraph.linkedin.com) for Brunei data inclusion — Brunei's LinkedIn user base (~100K+) may now be sufficient for national-level AI skills data generation; (2) AITI digital skills programme to publish AI skill development targets publicly — increasing Bruneians with AI certifications listed on LinkedIn; (3) AITI to mandate that AITI-funded AI upskilling graduates update LinkedIn profiles — creating a measurable signal for LinkedIn Economic Graph.

indicators.name: Use of virtual social networks
indicators.definition:
Measures extent to which businesses use virtual social networks (Facebook, Twitter, WeChat) for professional purposes. WEF EOS survey. Normalised 0–100.

Official GTCI Data Source: WEF Executive Opinion Survey 2025; reports.weforum.org
indicators.why_relevant: WEF EOS measures business use of virtual social networks for professional purposes — a proxy for digital business culture maturity. For the ICT sector, social network adoption for business signals uptake of digital channels for B2B communication, customer acquisition, and professional development. Brunei's 63.5/100 (rank 70) is moderate — WhatsApp Business, Facebook Business, and Instagram commerce are widely used — but LinkedIn and specialist B2B platforms are underutilised by ICT companies and professionals.
indicators.remarks: Score 63.5/100 (rank 70/135). ICT digital culture actions: (1) AITI's AITI digital skills programme to include 'Digital Marketing for Business' module covering LinkedIn, Google Ads, and e-commerce platforms; (2) ICT associations (BruDev, Brunei Digital) to actively use LinkedIn for industry networking and event promotion — increases WEF EOS respondents' perception of professional digital network use; (3) Darussalam Enterprise to require all registered SMEs to maintain a LinkedIn company page as a digital presence standard.

indicators.name: Use of virtual professional networks
indicators.definition:
Penetration of virtual professional networking platforms (e.g. LinkedIn) among the employed workforce, measured as share of employed persons with an active professional network profile.

Official GTCI Data Source: LinkedIn Economic Graph — Professional Network Penetration; economicgraph.linkedin.com
indicators.why_relevant: LinkedIn Economic Graph measures penetration of professional networking platforms among the employed workforce — for the ICT sector, LinkedIn penetration directly correlates with tech talent visibility, developer job matching, and ICT startup recruitment capacity. At 36.47/100 (rank 35), Brunei's LinkedIn penetration is moderate but needs to grow for the ICT sector to attract international tech talent and for Bruneian developers to be visible to global tech employers considering Brunei-based development operations.
indicators.remarks: Score 36.47/100 (rank 35/135). ICT talent visibility actions: (1) UBD, UTB, and IBTE to mandate LinkedIn profiles as graduation prerequisite — adds ~5,000 new complete Brunei profiles annually; (2) AITI AITI coding bootcamp graduates required to create and maintain GitHub + LinkedIn profiles — makes ICT talent internationally visible; (3) BEDB to publish Brunei's LinkedIn Talent Insights data (available through LinkedIn Partnership) — mapping supply of ICT skills and demand gaps for workforce planning.

indicators.name: Digital Skills
indicators.definition:
Composite score from ITU ICT Development Index (IDI) measuring digital access (network coverage, subscriptions), digital use (internet usage, data traffic), and digital skills (literacy rates, enrolment). Score 0–100 normalised.

Official GTCI Data Source: ITU ICT Development Index 2024; datahub.itu.int
indicators.why_relevant: ITU ICT Development Index (IDI) composite score measuring digital access (network coverage, subscriptions), digital use (internet usage, data traffic), and digital skills (literacy rates, enrolment). For the ICT sector, this is the definitive international benchmark of a country's digital ecosystem. Brunei's 100/100 (rank 1, tied with Singapore) reflects exceptional ICT infrastructure as captured by ITU — though the perfect score is partially an artefact of ITU's normalisation methodology and captures infrastructure quality more than actual workforce digital competency depth.
indicators.remarks: Score 100/100 (rank 1/135) — tied with Singapore. Important caveat: the perfect ITU score reflects infrastructure metrics, not workforce digital competency as measured by OECD PIAAC. Actions: (1) Commission Brunei National Digital Skills Assessment (AITI + MOE) using OECD PIAAC methodology or EU DIGCOMP framework — evidence-based picture of actual digital skills proficiency; (2) Submit comprehensive ITU data to DataHub (datahub.itu.int) annually through AITI to maintain rank 1; (3) Use ITU rank 1 in BEDB ICT sector investment marketing.

indicators.name: AI Talent Concentration
indicators.definition:
Share of AI-skilled workers — persons with AI-related skills on LinkedIn profiles — as a percentage of total LinkedIn members in a country, benchmarked globally. New indicator in GTCI 2025.

Official GTCI Data Source: LinkedIn Economic Graph AI Skills Report / OECD AI Policy Observatory; economicgraph.linkedin.com / oecd.ai
indicators.primary_source:
LinkedIn Economic Graph
OECD AI Policy Observatory
indicators.why_relevant: LinkedIn / OECD AI Policy Observatory measures share of AI-skilled professionals as % of the total workforce — a new GTCI 2025 indicator specifically designed to capture AI readiness. For Brunei's ICT and Digital Economy sector, AI talent concentration is the most forward-looking capability indicator: the ability to deploy, customise, and build AI applications determines whether Brunei becomes an AI consumer or an AI producer. N/A score means Brunei is currently invisible in global AI talent benchmarking frameworks.
indicators.remarks: Score N/A (new GTCI 2025 indicator — Brunei not covered). Priority actions: (1) Digital Economy Masterplan 2025 (DE25) AI talent targets should be tied to measurable LinkedIn certifications — target: 500 Brunei LinkedIn profiles with 'Machine Learning', 'Python', 'Data Science', 'Generative AI' skills by 2026; (2) AITI to engage OECD AI Policy Observatory (oecd.ai/en/countries) to ensure Brunei's AI policy data is captured — formal national AI strategy submission required; (3) UBD and UTB to launch accredited AI degree pathways — producing LinkedIn-verifiable AI graduates.

indicators.name: ICT Services Exports
indicators.definition:
Exports of ICT services (telecommunications, computer services, information services) as a percentage of total service exports. Measures productive capacity of the national ICT sector in internationally tradeable outputs.

Official GTCI Data Source: WIPO GII / WTO Trade in Commercial Services; stats.wto.org
indicators.primary_source:
Global Innovation Index Database, WIPO 2025
WTO nd UN Conference on Trade & Development, Trade in Commercial Services Database
indicators.why_relevant: WTO / WIPO GII measures ICT services exports (telecommunications, computer services, information services) as % of total service exports — the commercial output of the national ICT sector in internationally tradeable form. For Brunei's digital economy aspiration, a successful ICT sector must generate services (software development, IT consulting, cybersecurity services, digital BPO) that earn foreign exchange. Brunei's 2.44/100 (rank 112) reflects a minimal ICT export base while O&G dominates 90%+ of total exports.
indicators.remarks: Score 2.44/100 (rank 112/135). ICT export development actions: (1) BEDB to develop ICT Export Promotion Programme — identify Bruneian ICT companies with regional export potential and provide market entry support for Indonesia, Philippines, and Malaysia; (2) Ensure all ICT service export revenues are correctly captured in BOP data — BDCB to instruct banks to accurately classify ICT service receipts in BOP service account; (3) AITI to set ICT Services Export target: BND 50M by 2028, with annual tracking through BDCB BOP data.

indicators.name: Mobile App Development
indicators.definition:
Mobile application downloads originating from developers in a country per million population, normalised globally. Measures productivity and commercial output of the national mobile software development ecosystem.

Official GTCI Data Source: WIPO GII 2025 via Sensor Tower / data.ai — Mobile App Downloads per Capita; wipo.int/gii-ranking

Global Innovation Index Database, WIPO 2025
Sensor Tower Company (data.ia)
International Monetary Fund, World Economic Outlook Database
indicators.why_relevant: Sensor Tower / data.ai via WIPO GII measures mobile application downloads from apps developed in a country per million population. For Brunei's ICT sector, app development is a high-value-added digital economy activity employing developers, designers, and product managers — exactly the knowledge economy jobs AITI targets. Brunei's 43.27/100 (rank 112) reflects limited but existing app activity: BruHealth, SALPOP (government), and several private apps are published internationally.
indicators.remarks: Score 43.27/100 (rank 112/135). App ecosystem actions: (1) AITI AppSprint and StartUp Brunei competitions to target international app store submissions — every app on Google Play or Apple App Store with 'Brunei Darussalam' as origin country improves WIPO GII data; (2) Encourage BruDev community (brudevelopers.gov.bn) to publish open-source apps internationally; (3) AITI to include 'Apps Published to International Stores' as a KPI metric in the Digital Economy Masterplan.

indicators.definition:
GitHub repository creation activity (new repositories per million population, normalised). Measures depth and activity of a country's open-source software development community.

Official GTCI Data Source: WIPO GII 2025 via GitHub — Repository Activity per Capita; github.com / wipo.int/gii-ranking
indicators.primary_source:
GitHub via WIPO GII 2025 / old methodology
UN, Department of Economic and Social Affairs, Population Division, World Population Prospects 2024
indicators.why_relevant: GitHub repository activity per million population (via WIPO GII) measures open-source software development output — the globally recognised metric for developer ecosystem health used by international tech companies assessing where to locate development centres and hire engineers. Brunei's 3.02/100 (rank 96) reflects a small but growing developer community — BruHack, BruDev, and AITI bootcamp graduates are all contributors whose work is not yet systematically captured in WIPO/GitHub data.
indicators.remarks: Score 3.02/100 (rank 96/135). Developer ecosystem actions: (1) AITI AITI coding bootcamp graduates required to publish all bootcamp projects on GitHub under personal accounts with 'Brunei' affiliation — each public repository counted in WIPO GII data; (2) BruHack annual hackathon to require all team projects to be published as public GitHub repositories; (3) Government open-source mandate: all non-sensitive government ICT projects to publish code on GitHub under a 'Brunei Government' organisation account — modelled on Singapore's approach.

New limited liability companies registered per year per 1,000 working-age adults (15–64). Measures entrepreneurial dynamism and the ease of formal business creation.

Official GTCI Data Source: World Bank Entrepreneurship Snapshots Database; worldbank.org/en/programs/entrepreneurship

The score of 0/100 (rank 125) masks actual ROCBN data showing 6,570+ new registrations in 2023 — a pure data submission gap creating a false impression of a dormant ICT entrepreneurial ecosystem.

ICT-specific: (1) ROCBN to disaggregate new registrations by sector — giving BEDB and AITI annual data on ICT and digital economy start-up formation rates; (2) AITI to set a target of 100 new registered ICT companies annually by 2027 — tracked through ROCBN data and reported in AITI annual progress report.

indicators.name: Finding Skilled Employees
indicators.definition:
Measures ease of finding workers with required skills. WEF EOS: 'How easy is it for companies to find workers with the skills they require?' (1=extremely difficult; 7=extremely easy). Normalised 0–100.

Official GTCI Data Source: WEF Executive Opinion Survey 2025; reports.weforum.org
indicators.why_relevant: WEF EOS ease of finding skilled employees — for the ICT sector, this is the most critical growth constraint. Brunei's rank 119/135 directly captures what ICT employers report: near-impossibility of finding locally qualified software developers, cybersecurity professionals, AI engineers, data scientists, and ICT project managers. Without solving skills supply, Brunei's AITI Digital Economy ambitions are structurally blocked — international ICT companies will not invest where they cannot hire.
indicators.remarks: Score 42.88/100 (rank 119/135) — Brunei's worst indicator, most acute in ICT sector. ICT skills actions: (1) AITI Critical ICT Skills Inventory — annual survey of 50+ ICT employers identifying specific shortage roles (cybersecurity engineers, Python developers, cloud architects, UX/UI designers); (2) Fast-track ICT Skills Work Pass — 2-year permit for shortage ICT roles, processed within 5 business days by AITI; (3) New IBTE campus programme in network engineering, cybersecurity, and software development with guaranteed employer placement; (4) UBD/UTB Computer Science intakes to double by 2027.

indicators.name: Researchers, Full-time equivalent
indicators.definition:
Full-time equivalent (FTE) researchers per 1,000 persons in the labour force. Researchers are professionals engaged in creation of new knowledge, products, processes, methods and systems.

Official GTCI Data Source: UNESCO UIS Form R / WIPO GII / OECD MSTI — Researcher FTE Density; uis.unesco.org
indicators.primary_source:
Global Innovation Index Databas, WIPO 2025.
UNESCO Institute for Statistics (UIS)
OECD, Main Science and Technology Indicators (MSTI) Database
Ibero-American and Inter-American Network of Science and Technology Indicators (RICYT)
indicators.why_relevant: UNESCO FTE researcher density — for the ICT sector, researchers are the source of applied AI, cybersecurity, and software engineering innovations. A country without researchers cannot develop proprietary ICT technologies or compete in the global digital innovation economy. Brunei at 4.82/100 (rank 73) reflects fewer than 25 full-time researchers nationally — far below what is needed to build meaningful ICT R&D capability. Without researchers, Brunei's digital economy remains a technology consumer rather than a technology producer.
indicators.remarks: Score 4.82/100 (rank 73/135). ICT R&D capacity actions: (1) Establish UBD Cybersecurity Research Centre and AI Research Institute — each employing 10+ FTE researchers, directly improving UNESCO data; (2) AITI R&D Fund: BND 2M/year for ICT-focused applied research at UBD and UTB (cybersecurity, AI/ML, IoT, blockchain); (3) ICT Researcher Fast-Track Visa — 3-year research work pass for PhD-qualified AI and cybersecurity researchers, 10 business-day processing; (4) Submit complete researcher census to UNESCO UIS Form R (uis.stats@unesco.org) by June annually.

indicators.name: R&D Expenditure
indicators.definition:
Gross domestic expenditure on research and development (GERD) as a percentage of GDP. Covers basic research, applied research, and experimental development by all institutional sectors.

Official GTCI Data Source: UNESCO UIS R&D Survey (Form S); WIPO GII; OECD MSTI — uis.unesco.org
indicators.primary_source: UNESCO Institute for Statistics, WIPO GII, OECD MSTI
indicators.why_relevant: UNESCO GERD measure — for the ICT sector, R&D investment is the engine of digital innovation. Without public or private R&D funding, there are no new apps, no cybersecurity tools, no AI algorithms, no software products. Brunei's 4.29/100 (rank 80) reflects ~0.1% GDP in R&D vs Singapore's ~2.0% and Malaysia's ~1.4%. The Digital Economy Masterplan 2025 (DE25)'s innovation targets are structurally constrained by this underinvestment. ICT R&D specifically requires dedicated software development research funding alongside the O&G R&D agenda.
indicators.remarks: Score 4.29/100 (rank 80/135). ICT R&D investment actions: (1) Allocate 10% of AITI digital skills programme budget to UBD/UTB applied research in AI, cybersecurity, and digital services — estimated BND 2M/year; (2) AITI R&D Tax Credit: 150% deduction for private ICT sector R&D spending — joint proposal with MoFE; (3) Ensure all AITI, AITI, and government ICT R&D spending is captured in UNESCO UIS Form S (uis.stats@unesco.org) — submit by June annually; (4) Shell Brunei and BSP technology R&D to be included in national R&D expenditure reporting.

indicators.name: Scientific Journal Articles
indicators.definition:
Citable scientific journal articles (Scopus / Web of Science) per million population. Measures quantity of peer-reviewed knowledge output from a country's research system.

Official GTCI Data Source: UNESCO Science Report / SCImago–Scopus / World Bank WDI; uis.unesco.org
indicators.primary_source:
UNESCO Science Report / Web of Science / Scopus
World Bank World Development Indicators based on National Science Foundation, Science and Engineering Indicators
indicators.why_relevant: SCImago/Scopus measures peer-reviewed publications per million population — for the ICT sector, publications in computer science, AI, cybersecurity, and data science signal a country's contribution to global digital knowledge and attract international research partnerships. Brunei at 32.91/100 (rank 37) has improving research output led by UBD, but ICT-specific publications (computer science, information systems, electrical engineering) remain a small share. Building ICT research publication output is essential for attracting international tech research partners.
indicators.remarks: Score 32.91/100 (rank 37/135) — improving. ICT publications actions: (1) UBD Computer Science Department to target 50 Scopus-indexed ICT publications per year by 2027 (estimated current: ~10–15); (2) AITI R&D Fund to require all funded research to produce at least one peer-reviewed publication as grant condition; (3) Central APC Fund (BND 500,000/year) for open-access ICT journals (IEEE Access, Computers & Security, AI Communications); (4) UBD to pursue ICT co-publications with NUS School of Computing, RMIT Cybersecurity, and NTU School of Information Systems.

policies.title: Enterprise Digital Adoption
policies.details: Submit national ICT market data to ITU (datahub.itu.int) annually. Launch enterprise digitalisation initiatives through AITI (aiti.gov.bn). Reference Statista Technology Market Outlook (statista.com/outlook/tmo) for benchmarking. National cloud-first policy and SME digital adoption support needed.

policies.title: AI Talent & Skills
policies.details: Register Brunei's Digital Economy Masterplan 2025 (DE25) on OECD AI Policy Observatory (oecd.ai/en/countries — free registration). Contact LinkedIn Economic Graph (economicgraph.linkedin.com) for Brunei AI skills data inclusion. Grow LinkedIn user base through university graduation requirements.

policies.title: ICT Services Exports
policies.details: Ensure BDCB (bdcb.gov.bn) correctly classifies ICT services exports in BOP submissions to IMF (imf.org/en/Data) under EBOPS Category 10. Develop Brunei ICT export strategy targeting ASEAN markets. Establish digital free zone for ICT exporters.

policies.title: Software & App Development
policies.details: Encourage open-source development on GitHub (github.com) — WIPO GII (wipo.int/gii-ranking/en) uses GitHub commit data. Run public hackathons with open-source code outputs. Establish government open-source code repository.

policies.title: New Business Formation for ICT SMEs
policies.details: Submit ROCBN/OCP (ocp.mofe.gov.bn) enterprise data to World Bank Entrepreneurship Database (worldbank.org/en/programs/entrepreneurship). ICT sector SME formation is constrained by low new business density score. Simplify digital business registration to 1 day.

policies.title: World Bank Enterprise Surveys Inclusion
policies.details: Request World Bank Enterprise Surveys (enterprisesurveys.org) inclusion for Brunei. This directly measures firms' digital adoption (website use, cloud use, e-commerce). Brunei not surveyed since 2015 — both 1.3.7 and 3.2.2 are zero as a result.

intl_orgs.name: ITU DataHub
intl_orgs.role: AITI (aiti.gov.bn) submits annual ICT questionnaire. Feeds digital skills (6.1.6) and 3G coverage (1.2.5). Deadline 31 January annually.

intl_orgs.name: LinkedIn Economic Graph
intl_orgs.role: Contact for Brunei AI skills data inclusion. Feeds 2.1.6 (AI Skills Migration) and 6.1.7 (AI Talent Concentration).

intl_orgs.name: OECD AI Policy Observatory
intl_orgs.role: Register AITI Masterplan — free, immediate. Establishes Brunei in global AI policy tracking. Feeds 6.1.7.

intl_orgs.name: WIPO GII
intl_orgs.role: Feeds mobile app (6.2.2), software development (6.2.5), and ICT exports (6.2.1). Submit R&D data through UNESCO UIS which WIPO uses.

intl_orgs.name: World Bank Enterprise Surveys
intl_orgs.role: Request inclusion to fix 1.3.7 and 3.2.2 (both zero, not surveyed since 2015). One inclusion request fixes both indicators.

intl_orgs.name: IMF Data
intl_orgs.role: BDCB (bdcb.gov.bn) BOP submission — ensure ICT service exports are correctly classified. Feeds 6.2.1.
